Se concluye que el obstáculo chileno no reside en la propiedad de la firma —la colegiatura es voluntaria desde 1981 y no rige una prohibición equivalente a la Regla 5.4— sino en el monopolio personal de la postulación (Ley N° 18.120, arts. 1 y 2), reforzado por sanción penal (art. 3) y por el carácter intuitu personae del título de abogado (arts. 521 y 527 del Código Orgánico de Tribunales). La ficción de la «firma-IA» no es trasplantable; sí lo es, en cambio, su función legítima: la inteligencia artificial como herramienta de preparación bajo patrocinio, firma y responsabilidad de un abogado habilitado. https://zenodo.org/records/20848233

title: A HISTÓRIA ECONÔMICA NO BRASIL
abstract: Este artigo analisa aproximadamente 50 mil teses e dissertações defendidas em departamentos de história e economia ao longo das últimas quatro décadas, com o objetivo de mapear a evolução da história econômica no Brasil e oferecer aos interessados um guia para o desenvolvimento da área. Antes da análise empírica, centrada no período 1980-2022, apresento um panorama histórico que cobre desde o surgimento dos estudos de caráter ensaístico, predominantes até a década de 1950, até o amadurecimento da produção acadêmica. Esse processo teve início na década de 1960, com a contribuição dos “brasilianistas”, e consolidou-se a partir dos anos 1980, quando a expansão dos programas de pós-graduação em diferentes regiões do país firmou a pesquisa monográfica e especializada como padrão dominante da disciplina.

title: The Measure Makes the Finding: Racial disparity, democracy, and punishment in the U.S. states
abstract: This paper asks whether American states with healthier democracies treat Black and white residents more equally in their prisons, and finds that the answer is mostly an artifact of how the gap is measured. The usual measure, the Black/White imprisonment ratio, runs higher in more democratic states. Taken at face value, that says democratic health and racial fairness in punishment work against each other. But the ratio is higher there only because those states imprison white residents so rarely, not because they imprison Black residents more. By the absolute Black rate, those states are no worse, even a little better. The paper carries the same question through three further choices an analyst barely notices, comparing states against each other rather than against their own past, sorting them into trajectory types, and reading Florida's 2018 felon re-enfranchisement by its text against its turnout. Each choice moves the answer. A single Bayesian model of the underlying imprisonment rates then derives the competing measures as contrasts of one posterior, so their disagreement is reported as a probability, not a row of conflicting numbers. In a field that quantifies racial injustice with ratios and gaps, the measure of disparity is often the finding.

title: Learning interdependence: youth arts as relational skills in Singapore
abstract: This article examines how arts-based co-curricular activities (CCAs) in Singaporean higher education navigate the tension between state pragmatism and student experience. Situated within the SkillsFuture framework and a meritocratic ethos that instrumentalises education for economic productivity, arts programmes can be viewed as sites for acquiring ‘21st-century skills’. Drawing on qualitative research with university students involved in a range of arts CCAs this study explores the relationship between official discourses of individual skill accumulation and students' subjective accounts of these skills. While students acknowledge acquiring transferable competencies, their narratives highlight a distinct process of learning and working through interdependence. Rather than fostering individualised self-optimisation, arts CCAs become spaces where young people collaboratively negotiate cultural difference, adapt to diverse perspectives, and build relational capacity. The findings suggest that while students embody the contradictory pressures of neoliberal governance – balancing anxieties about career viability with a desire for ‘fun’ – the arts cultivate a critical, reflexive intersubjectivity. This embodied experience of interdependence offers a complex, collective response to diversity that challenges the atomised logic of labour market agendas, and highlights the need for accreditation frameworks that acknowledge students’ experiences and values.

title: Le res novae tra questione operaia e intelligenza artificiale. Un confronto lessicometrico esplorativo tra Rerum novarum (1891) e Magnifica humanitas (2026)
abstract: A centotrentacinque anni dalla Rerum novarum, la Magnifica humanitas di Leone XIV si apre richiamando le “res novae del nostro tempo” e si colloca esplicitamente nel solco della prima grande enciclica sociale. Il presente contributo, di carattere esplorativo, confronta per via lessicometrica i due testi per descrivere che cosa muti, sul piano del lessico, nel passaggio dalla questione operaia a quella dell’intelligenza artificiale, impiegando keyness con misura dell’effetto, analisi dei campi semantici, ricchezza lessicale e controllo qualitativo mediante concordanze. Emerge una continuità di parte del lessico morale, con frequenze comparabili per giustizia e carità, e un marcato spostamento di enfasi, dal vocabolario di lavoro e proprietà a quello di tecnica, persona, dignità e custodia. La lettura ravvicinata e un corpus di controllo di quattro documenti magisteriali recenti (Laborem exercens, Laudato si’, Fratelli tutti, Antiqua et nova) ridimensionano però l’apparente novità dell’enciclica: il calo del lemma natura non è abbandono del giusnaturalismo, ma variazione di registro; il lessico personalista e tecnologico è eredità del magistero recente che l’enciclica condensa; la sua nota più riconoscibile, la preferenza per il termine custodia riferito alla persona, non corrisponde a un primato del più ampio campo della cura, dove prevale la Laudato si’. Il contributo è soprattutto metodologico: il caso mostra che la lessicometria applicata ai testi magisteriali, senza vaglio qualitativo, genera falsi positivi, e che i conteggi vanno verificati con concordanze, controllo sulle lingue d’origine e un corpus di riferimento.

title: NUEVAS SUBJETIVIDADES EN TORNO A LA FERTILIZACIÓN ASISTIDA. DERECHOS HUMANOS Y BIOÉTICA.
abstract: En el presente trabajo se analiza el derecho a la vida y a la salud, en relación a la decisión de formar una familia, haciendo foco en la procreación asistida. A tal fin se describen algunos de los resultados arrojados por una investigación que realizamos en la UNMDP. La investigación se realizó por medio de entrevistas a profesionales psicólogos de instituciones de salud en Mar del Plata. Las dimensiones analizadas que abordaremos son la complementación en la implementación de las nuevas leyes y el saber psicológico, y la ponderación psicológica de las nuevas subjetividades que surgen de ejercer la autonomía en el ámbito del nacer. A través del diálogo y la competencia comunicativa se puede dar cuenta desde la ética, del reconocimiento y respeto de la autonomía del paciente. Respetando así la dignidad de la persona, a fin de no caer en la cosificación del otro. En este sentido es fundamental la concepción de Autonomía de la voluntad, que expresa la Ley N° 26742, sobre Derechos del Paciente en su relación con los profesionales e instituciones de la salud, y la Ley 26529 sancionada por medio de la Ley N° 26742, en articulación con la Ley 26862 de Fertilización asistida. Se procura un abordaje crítico, realizado con un enfoque bioético y se analiza la cuestión desde la perspectiva de Derechos Humanos. Para lo cual se parte desde una breve conceptualización de los mismos. Finalmente se plantean las dificultades y posibilidades de realizar cambios históricos en el desarrollo de nuevas subjetividades. Ponderando la necesidad de continuar investigando y analizando la problemática en cuestión desde el paradigma de la complejidad, pasando de la multidisciplinariedad a la interdisciplinariedad y transdisciplinariedad.

title: Pattern Recognition of Zakat Optimization Potential Through Harmonization
abstract: The administration of Zakat in Malaysia operates as a highly sophisticated, multi-state macroeconomic engine serving a vital role in community empowerment and socioeconomic harmonization. However, its true systemic penetration and purchasing power are frequently obscured by fragmented state-level policies, varying regional thresholds, and data incompleteness. Despite nominal collections reaching record highs, the real socio-economic utility of these funds risks erosion due to underlying compliance and extraction models that are misaligned with inflation and national GDP. This paper bridges Islamic socio-finance with advanced data science to transition the ecosystem from traditional static accounting to algorithmic optimization, charting high-yield productivity vectors across demographic, asset, and corporate domains.

title: The Breadth and Depth of Opposition to Antiracism: How Resistance to Antiracist Claims and Activism Shapes White Americans’ Political Attitudes
abstract: Previous scholarship has most often examined White Americans’ opposition to antiracist claims, policies, and movements as a dependent variable, shaped by forces such as racial prejudice and Whites’ group interests. However, little research has examined the consequences of these attitudes, despite recent events suggesting the importance of these views in conservative mobilization. Across two surveys and three experiments, we explore how opposition to antiracism (or anti-antiracism) shapes the political views of White Americans (total N=6,495). We find anti-antiracism has broad endorsement and effects: it is prevalent among Whites and is a powerful predictor of many policy positions, including being more strongly related to support for Donald Trump than several measures of racial prejudice or political ideology. At the same time, anti-antiracism is especially strongly related with Trump-related attitudes among religious, conservative, White Republicans (Studies 1a-b). Next, across two pre-registered survey-experiments (Studies 2a-b), we find anti-antiracism predicts Whites’ responses to policy frames and political candidates that invoke issues of race. Finally, we find opposition to antiracism is resistant to counterevidence: a factual intervention correcting misperceptions of anti-Black hiring discrimination influenced perceived rates of discrimination, but did not influence more general anti-antiracist attitudes (Study 3). These findings suggest opposition to antiracist claims and activism is a powerful coalitional resource for the Republican Party, allowing them to draw broad-based support while activating an even stronger response from their base, via attitudes that persist in the face of counter-evidence.

title: The Constituency for Personal Politicians: Social Embeddedness and Voter Behavior in Indonesia
authors: Harry Janos Dienes, Burhanuddin Muhtadi, Hui Yuan Neo, Eitan Paul
abstract: Do voters support personal intervention by politicians to facilitate access to public services? Does contingency – the defining characteristic of clientelism – matter? We conduct a survey experiment to test Indonesian voter reactions to three political service delivery strategies: impartial bureaucratic monitoring, personal intervention for all constituents, and clientelistic intervention for supporters only. Personal intervention is punished compared to programmatic monitoring, and contingency generates no further electoral costs than non-partisan personal intervention. Yet through a candidate survey, we find elected candidates make deals for access to services more often than unelected ones. To reconcile these findings, we show that social embeddedness moderates reactions. Voters with low social density and those who have approached politicians or bureaucrats for help find personal or clientelistic strategies as appealing as programmatic. These groups constitute a (minority) constituency for personal intervention as a legitimate electoral strategy. The results emphasize social relationships in voter reactions to clientelism.

title: Fragmented Identity, Saffron Strategy: Navigating the Bharatiya Janata Party's Ascendance in Bodoland Territorial Region (BTR) of Assam
authors: Bikash Dihingia, Chuchengfa Gogoi, Pankaj Kumar Sarmah
abstract: The unprecedented rise of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) in the 2020 election to the Bodoland Territorial Region (BTR), erstwhile Bodoland Territorial Council (BTC) of Assam, marked a significant development in the region's political landscape, which is historically dominated by the Bodoland People’s Front (BPF), an ethnic political party representing the Bodos. For decades, the BPF exercised near-hegemonic control over the BTC, shaping governance structures and political dialogue through an ethnic-centric approach. The fourth BTR election disrupted this established order and brought in a more competitive and pluralistic political environment. The shift from single-party dominance to an emerging multi-party system not only reconfigured electoral alignments but also contested the institutional and ideological bases of BPF’s authority. This study thoroughly examines the growing dynamics of electoral politics in the BTR, positioning the 2020 election as a decisive departure from conventional patterns of political mobilisation and voter behaviour. It also explores how changing socio-political conditions, including internal differentiations within the Bodo community, generated new opportunities for political contestation. Particular attention is given to the BJP’s strategic interventions, especially its capacity to leverage fragmented ethnic identities and integrate them within a wider electoral framework. Through this analysis, the study intends to advance understanding of shifting political narratives, party competition, and identity negotiations in contemporary Bodoland.

title: E-Commerce Challenges and Opportunities in Turkey: The Cases of Getir and Trendyol
abstract: This article explores the challenges and opportunities of e-commerce in Turkey through inducting a comparison between Getir and Trendyol cases. With the fast expansion of digital technologies, e-commerce has reformed consumer habits, business strategies, and the retail landscape. based on secondary data from company reports, industry analysis, and academic studies, this research follows a qualitative case study approach to evaluate how both companies achieved market growth while dealing with operational and regulatory pressures and challenges. Findings reveal that e-commerce platforms create essential opportunities, including consumer convenience, market expansion, and innovation-driven growth. At the same time, they face significant challenges such as high operational costs, supply chain issues, legal and regulatory issues, and economic instability due to current conditions. The analysis shows that Getir’s ultra-fast delivery model enabled early growth but came with sustainability concerns, while Trendyol’s marketplace model ensured greater scalability but faced increasing competition. Overall, the study contributes to understanding how e-commerce businesses in emerging markets adapt strategies to sustain growth within dynamic conditions. It further provides practical insights for policymakers, businesses, and startups on building sustainable, competitive, and consumer-centered e-commerce ecosystems.

title: Lame Duck by Primary: Effects of Electoral Incentives on U.S. House Representatives
abstract: How do electoral incentives motivate representatives to exert effort and shape policy positions? While reelection is widely recognized as representatives' primary motivation in democratic politics, accurately estimating its effects is challenging because representatives self-select into rerunning or retiring decisions. Leveraging legislators’ lame duck status after primary elections, this article estimates how electoral incentives affect U.S. House Representatives. Compiling data from 1955 to 2022, I compare legislators who win primaries and remain motivated for reelection to those who lose and exit the race. Through regression discontinuity and difference-in-differences designs, I find that primary losers significantly reduce effort-related activities, as evidenced by decreased roll-call participation, bill sponsorship, and voter communication. In contrast, the results show that legislators do not adjust policy positions for reelection; both winners and losers maintain consistent ideological and party-line adherence. These findings illuminate the scope of electoral incentives' effects and contribute to debates on electoral accountability and democratic representation.

title: When Formal Sanctions Synchronize: A Multi-Level Model of Organizational Defensive Escalation
abstract: This paper offers a theoretical account of how internal problem-raising and workplace conflict may shift into chains of formal procedures and sanctions. Prior research has examined organizational defensiveness through institutional legitimacy, threat-rigidity, defensive routines, psychological safety, whistleblower retaliation, and organizational silence. These studies have shown that organizations often respond to internal disturbance by narrowing attention, reinforcing procedures, and suppressing voice. Less attention, however, has been paid to how interactional control at the individual level becomes connected with formal organizational procedures at the institutional level. To address this issue, the paper reconsiders the Formality–Sanction Loop (FS-Loop) as a multi-level process. At the interpersonal level, an individual FS-Loop operates through the detection of minor formal deviations, criticism, demands for apology or reflection, and confirmation of compliance. Such interactions may reduce the target’s capacity to speak or act autonomously. At the organizational level, an institutional FS-Loop operates when internal problem-raising is redefined less as a substantive issue than as a matter of procedure, documentation, attitude, or order, and is then processed through meetings, records, warnings, disciplinary procedures, or reassignment. The central argument is that organizational defensive escalation becomes more likely when these two loops synchronize. A local judgment that a person is insufficiently reflective or in need of correction may support formal organizational procedures, while organizational records and sanctions may reinforce the local judgment that the person is problematic. Through this mutual reinforcement, the substantive issue recedes into the background, while the person’s attitude, procedural compliance, documentary record, and willingness to comply become the main focus. The paper positions FS-Loop synchronization as a middle-range framework for examining harassment, whistleblower retaliation, organizational silence, disciplinary procedures, and the erosion of psychological safety. Rather than reducing such cases either to individual hostility or institutional failure alone, the model highlights how locally reasonable actions may become connected across levels and produce defensive closure.

title: India’s Economic Crossroads: Growth, Inequality, Dependency, and the Road Ahead
abstract: India has re‑emerged as one of the world’s fastest‑growing large economies, yet persistent concerns about job quality, inequality, and strategic dependence raise doubts about how far headline growth translates into durable development. This paper asks whether India can convert high growth into inclusive, job‑rich, and strategically resilient development in a frontier‑technology era defined by AI, semiconductors, digital public infrastructure, and advanced defence and data‑centre technologies. Using a secondary‑data, mixed‑methods design, the study combines national time series, a state‑year panel, and trade and energy‑dependence indicators constructed from official Indian sources (MoSPI, RBI, NITI Aayog, PPAC, TradeStat, NPCI) and major international databases (World Bank, IMF, WID). The analysis documents that while real output and fiscal‑backed investment have risen strongly, labour reallocation into high‑productivity manufacturing and modern services remains incomplete, so growth has been output‑rich but only partially employment‑rich across most states. Official consumption data show substantial poverty reduction, but inequality remains high and multi‑dimensional, with rising top‑end concentration and enduring gaps by state, caste, gender, and employment type. External vulnerability is concentrated in oil, electronics, and advanced hardware and software inputs, even as digital public infrastructure and frontier‑tech sectors deepen. The paper argues that India’s long‑run challenge is not growth per se but the political‑economy task of aligning structural transformation, capability‑building, and technology‑era industrial and social policy so that high growth yields broad‑based mobility, lower strategic dependence, and stronger, more even state capacity.

title: Governing Epistemic Sustainability: Epistemic Enclosure, Double Concealment, and Knowledge Sovereignty in India's Viksit Bharat 2047
authors: S Anas Ahmad, Mohammad Anas, DR. ABDULLAH NOUMAN, Filza Zaki Khan
abstract: India's Viksit Bharat 2047 framework (2026) and the IndiaAI Mission (2024) conflate compute sovereignty with knowledge sovereignty, embedding India within AI infrastructure whose epistemic consequences existing sustainability governance cannot register. Reading India's AI governance corpus as performative political narratives, the paper identifies the rhetorical operations through which compute capacity is narrated as knowledge sovereignty, and the silences through which epistemic costs are rendered external to the governance frame. To name what this discourse cannot see, the paper develops three interlocking concepts, each addressing a different question: what must be sustained, what happens to knowledge under enclosure, and why that enclosure persists ungoverned. Epistemic sustainability is the capacity of a knowledge system to remain durable, plural, and transmissible across time without structural dependence on extractive, proprietary, or epistemically homogenising infrastructure, extending Scheman's (2012) norm from the ethics of inquiry to the political economy of knowledge infrastructure. Epistemic enclosure names the mechanism: the print-to-digital-to-AI trajectory, narrated as democratising, renders postcolonial knowledge traditions legible only on terms they did not set, through architectures optimised for English-language statistical patterns. Indian languages account for approximately 0.5 per cent of Common Crawl data, a major source corpus for language-model training; traditions including Nyaya, Mimamsa, and Ayurveda are rendered legible through patent classification, formal logic, and metadata standards external to their conditions of intelligibility. Double concealment names the ideological operation sustaining this enclosure: material infrastructural costs are externalised from green AI accounting, while the foreclosure of non-Western epistemic plurality is rendered invisible behind narratives of democratised access. The paper proposes three governance instruments: epistemic impact assessments for public AI infrastructure investment; a policy framework distinguishing compute from knowledge sovereignty; and multilateral mechanisms protecting the epistemic commons of postcolonial traditions in global AI regimes. As India approaches its 2047 centenary, the governance question its AI discourse has not yet asked is whether the knowledge it produces will remain answerable to India's plural epistemic traditions, or merely processed through infrastructures designed elsewhere.

title: Beyond the Party: Campaign Visit Effects in Indonesia’s 2024 Election
authors: Harry Janos Dienes, Jahen Rezki, Burhanuddin Muhtadi, Afrimadona
abstract: Evidence on campaign visits originates from established democracies, yet many democracies feature weak party attachments, personalism, and fluid coalitions that might strengthen effects. We combine a hand-collected, geocoded dataset of visits by Indonesia’s incumbent president with pooled data from 150 campaign surveys. Our data permit distinguishing effects across time and across types of electoral strategies. Comparing respondents near visit dates, we estimate a short 7pp increase in voting intention for the president’s chosen successor, driven by visibly distributing handouts or interacting with residents. These effects are transient: even in a case favourable to personalist persuasion, there is no impact after two weeks or on the final election result, for any type.

title: Gobernanza deliberativa y salud pública
abstract: Este ensayo aborda los conceptos básicos de gobernanza deliberativa aplicada a la salud pública. Se plantea que los procesos de gobernanza son de doble filo. Por una parte, brindan la oportunidad de ampliar la participación ciudadana en asuntos públicos, como es el caso de los Consejos de Participación en Salud en Brasil. Otros enfoques de gobernanza propenden por un Estado mínimo que favorece alianzas público-privadas entre agencias gubernamentales e industrias como el tabaco, alcohol y comestibles ultraprocesados, en detrimento de la salud pública. Se plantea que la gobernanza deliberativa ofrece una perspectiva que concilia la urgencia de tomar acciones en asuntos relacionados con la salud pública con el imperativo de reconocer a la ciudadanía como autora de sus propias normas.

title: What works in international health worker recruitment: a megastudy
authors: Florian Keppeler, Elizabeth Linos, Brenda Sciepura, Rune Godske Bachmann, Karalyn Lacey, Christian Bøtcher Jacobsen
abstract: Health worker shortages are straining healthcare systems worldwide, yet there is limited causal evidence on how to recruit healthcare workers across national borders. In collaboration with Danish healthcare providers, we randomly assign 110,000 European nurses to receive one of 10 different digital messages (or no message), encouraging them to apply for a nursing job in Denmark. We selected interventions using an approach we call “community-led intervention design” (CID) that systematically balances academics’, practitioners’, and healthcare workers’ expertise. Contrary to expert predictions and prevailing policy guidance, messages highlighting pay decreased overall engagement by 7.7% (1.7 percentage points), positive responses by 6.3% (1.4 pp), and applications by 19.0% (0.15 pp) compared to baseline. Messages emphasizing social support and job benefits outperformed pay messages, increasing engagement by 8.1% (1.67 pp) and 9.6% (1.97 pp) respectively. Evidence from an additional online experiment (N = 1,799) suggests that highlighting pay may reduce credibility and signal a less desirable job in dimensions such as work-life balance or prosociality. This megastudy has far-reaching implications for international labor market decision-making, healthcare workforce policy, and hypothesis generation in behavioral science.

title: Indirect Influence: How Elite Attacks on Information Providers Affect Public Opinion Formation
authors: Erik Peterson, Allison M. N. Archer, Kishan Bhakta, Sho Izumisawa
abstract: Attacks from politicians can dramatically reduce public trust in sources of expertise and information. We consider the consequences of such criticism and argue that by shaping which information providers their supporters consider credible, politicians can exert indirect influence over public opinion formation. In two survey experiments that link elite criticism of the media to other outcomes, we show politicians who discredit a media outlet cause their supporters to avoid the targeted news source and perceive more bias in its coverage. Partisan attacks also reduce the subsequent effect of the news source's coverage on public opinion, although people are unable to fully discount information from sources they deem untrustworthy due to this rhetoric. Looking beyond direct means of elite influence such as positional cues and policy arguments, we show how harming the reputations of information providers allows politicians to indirectly influence the views of their supporters.

title: Mandatory AI Disclosure Sorts Products into Launch Failure: Evidence from Steam
abstract: Platforms increasingly require firms to disclose when products incorporate generative artificial intelligence (AI). Existing evidence largely asks whether such disclosure changes consumer demand; we show that a complementary question—where demand is lost—is at least as important. Using a cross-section of 799 visual-novel games on Steam released early enough to observe a complete 90-day post-release window—of which 22% carry a platform-mandated AI-use disclosure—we show that the consumer response to AI disclosure is concentrated almost entirely on the extensive margin. Disclosed games are roughly twice as likely to obtain no early traction at all: the rate of zero day-30 reviews rises from 5.0% to 9.0%, an odds ratio of 2.14. Among games that do gain an audience, by contrast, the rate of subsequent review accumulation is statistically indistinguishable by disclosure status, and the continuous accumulation penalty is not robust to controls. Disclosed games that are reviewed receive ratings about six percentage points lower. The market response to disclosure is therefore not a uniform discount applied to every product, but a bimodal pattern: a higher probability of failing to launch, and modestly lower satisfaction among survivors. We discuss implications for the design of mandatory disclosure more broadly: when a label can determine whether a product is seen at all, the welfare consequences of disclosure differ sharply depending on whether it gently discounts demand or sorts products into launch failure, and on the producer incentives it sets in motion.

title: High resolution measures of preference-based local segregation for the United States, 2018-2023
authors: Jiwon Jang, Dylan Connor, Peter Rogerson, Daoqin Tong
abstract: Neighborhood segregation is a strong driver of disparities in human well-being, yet traditional measures largely ignore the segregation experienced beyond residential neighborhoods. Recent human mobility-based measures have advanced this field by quantifying segregated mobility patterns when choosing travel destinations, but they are often confounded by spatial exposure (i.e., the demographic diversity of the neighborhoods locally reachable from one’s residence) rather than a behavioral preference for similarity. Without adjusting for this baseline exposure, such metrics largely act as proxies for the local residential environment, conflating limited demographic diversity in surrounding areas with active travel decisions to neighborhoods similar to one’s own. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Community Connectivity (CO-CONNECT) dataset, combining billions of anonymized cellphone location records with census tract-level socio-demographic attributes (2018-2023). The dataset operationalizes the Spatial Preference in Local Interaction (SPLIT) index, a local measure of segregation that quantifies the extent to which residents travel to socio-demographically similar neighborhoods, adjusted for the demographic similarity of surrounding neighborhoods. This framework explicitly separates travel decisions that exacerbate group-based segregation from segregation attributable to demographic composition alone, providing a high-resolution picture of mobility-based segregation and its temporal trends across the United States.

title: The Suburb Disruption Cycle and Dorsett’s Law of Suburban Succession: A Theoretical Model of Residential Turnover and Suburban Reinvention
abstract: This paper proposes the Suburb Disruption Cycle as a theoretical model for explaining suburban transformation as a patterned process of disruption, reorganisation and residential succession. Existing accounts of suburban change often emphasise particular dimensions of transformation, including decline, gentrification, densification, migration, financialisation, post-suburbanisation and symbolic revaluation. This paper synthesises these dimensions into an eight-stage causal sequence: Current Residents, Disruption, Housing Form, Affordability, Reputation, Business Ecology, Resident Turnover and New Residents. From this sequence, the paper derives Dorsett’s Law of Suburban Succession: sustained disruption within or upon a suburb produces residential succession when it reorganises housing form, affordability, reputation and business ecology. The law is advanced as a conditional sociological proposition rather than a deterministic claim. Its purpose is to explain how new resident orders are produced through cumulative material, economic, symbolic and commercial change. The paper develops the model conceptually and applies it to St Kilda, Victoria, during its Riviera of Victoria life cycle from 1860 to 1910. The case demonstrates how transport access, gold-rush wealth, land speculation and financial crisis contributed to housing transformation, rising exclusivity, reputational elevation, leisure-oriented business development and the emergence of a new elite seaside resident order. The paper contributes a middle-range theory of suburban reinvention that can guide future historical, comparative and empirical research into suburban succession.

title: When Are Democracies Under Strain? Supranational Economic Integration, Financial Crises, and Satisfaction with Democracy
abstract: Why does external financial support erode democratic satisfaction in some countries but leave it intact in others? This article argues that the answer lies in the depth of supranational economic integration. Where integration is limited, conditionality releases information about government performance: elections retain their accountability function and democratic satisfaction remains stable or recovers. Where integration is deep, conditionality releases systemic information: citizens learn that elections are uninformative about future policy direction, and satisfaction falls. This integration gradient hypothesis predicts that the sign, magnitude, and mechanism of conditionality's democratic effects vary continuously with integration depth. Across five cases spanning the continuum --- Colombia and South Korea at the low-integration pole, Hungary at an intermediate position, and the Eurozone and Portugal at the high-integration pole --- the evidence is consistent with the gradient. What appears as contradictory evidence in the literature is the theoretically predicted pattern of a single mechanism at different integration positions.

title: Unexpected Journeys: Russian Migrants Navigating Education and Social Spaces in the European Union
abstract: This study aims to identify how recent Russian migrant families navigate the European social space and conceptualize their newly acquired social position. It investigates the educational strategies and social integration of recent Russian middle-class migrant families within the European Union. Utilizing Bourdieu’s theoretical framework of social space and capital, the research explores how these families navigate their new social environments and conceptualize their social positions post-migration. For this qualitative analysis, data collection was conducted using semi-structured in-depth interviews as the primary research technique. The data was then thoroughly processed and analyzed using the tools proposed by Laclau and Mouffe’s discourse theory. The findings reveal that these families were among the select few in the high-income strata in Russia and now experience downward mobility upon relocating to the social space of European countries. Though this group of migrants cannot be fully conceptualized as the Global Middle Class's representatives, their actions align with the aspiration to sustain and elevate their social position. Their incomes may remain constant or even increase, yet they transition from select groups to the European middle class. Although this appears to be downward mobility, it is a strategic move to reinforce the family’s social standing within the global social space.

title: From crime survey items to measurement: Developing the perceived (un)safety scale
abstract: Purpose: This study examines the ability of a new collection of survey items, the perceived (un)safety scale, to function as a coherent invariant scale. Its secondary aim is to investigate how the perceived (un)safety scale can be used to identify associations with perceived unsafety across groups. Methods: Employing a cross-sectional psychometric design, data were collected through a postal survey in four Swedish municipal districts, differentiated by size and sociodemographic composition (n = 1,387), and a nationwide panel sample (n = 1,340). With the intention of measuring avoidance behaviours and detours due to fear of crime, the preliminary scale comprised five items. Rasch analysis was used to evaluate these measurements, and regression analyses were conducted to examine associations with individual characteristics. Results: Initial analyses identified one misfitting item and local dependency between two items. After removing the misfitting item and combining the locally dependent items into a testlet, a unidimensional and invariant scale was obtained. Higher levels of perceived (un)safety were associated with being a woman, being younger, and having a history of victimization. Conclusions: The perceived (un)safety scale provides a coherent and invariant measure of perceived (un)safety across demographic groups. The scale may be useful for future research, population surveys, and evaluations of interventions aimed at improving safety and reducing fear of crime.

title: When Central Banks Go Green: Public Opinion and Monetary Policy Legitimacy
authors: Björn Bremer, Jeffrey Chwieroth, Anita Mancosu
abstract: Climate change presents unelected central banks with an acute dilemma: acting on climate change risks charges of mandate overreach, while failing to act risks charges of institutional failure. Yet public opinion on green central banking remains un- studied. We address this gap with two survey experiments in Germany and the Netherlands. A conjoint experiment shows that adding environmental condition- ality to corporate bond purchases significantly increases support for quantitative easing, suggesting that greening can rehabilitate contested unconventional policies. A framing experiment reveals that institutional objections about price stability and democratic legitimacy erode support more than distributive objections about job losses or asset devaluation, inverting expectations from the climate politics literature. Respondents who trust the central bank most are paradoxically most responsive to arguments for and against it. Public trust in delegated institutions is thus not a blank check; it is a license granted on terms, policed most attentively by the institution’s own supporters.

title: Safeguards, Strategic Behavior, and Great Power Competition: The IAEA’s Board of Governors as an Instrument of Leverage for Potential Non- Compliers
authors: Ralph Rotte, Victoria Alberty, Andreas Dürholt, René Geiser
abstract: This paper takes a critical view of the prospective ability of the IAEA to be an essential contributor to a stable international nuclear order and prevention of nuclear proliferation, given that it is an international governmental organization. The Agency is considered to be characterized by the ambiguities of a more or less autonomous bureaucratic and technocratic organization led by the Secretariat. Furthermore, it is considered an intergovernmental arena of state actors for decision-making on the political consequences of breaking Safeguards rules with the Boards of Governors as a quasi-upstream body of the UN Security Council. As is typical for IGOs one can therefore expect it to ultimately be an instrument of states’ foreign policy interests which reflects the overall situation of the international system and its prevalent power structures and conflicts. Especially in times of power transition and a revisionist dissolution of an established international order this may lead to rivalling great powers’ acting according to their strategic priorities and protecting states that fail to fully comply with nuclear safeguards obligations, instead of providing for effective implementation of non-proliferation measures. As a consequence, this instrumentalization of the IAEA policy making bodies by leading powers’ strategic interests might enable unreliable countries to avoid diplomatic pressure or sanctions for clandestine cheating or even to whitewash their nuclear programs. By looking at the cases of Iran, South Korea, Egypt and Libya, we find that alliance politics and strategic behavior may indeed hamper the effectiveness of the IAEA as the global nuclear watchdog. Consequently, at least from a realist and strategic studies perspective, the IAEA and the nuclear nonproliferation regime face an ever-increasing structural challenge in the current context of aggravating great power competition.

title: Disproportionate Psychiatric Hospitalization of Black American Adults and the Role of Diagnostic Disparities
abstract: Disproportionate psychiatric hospitalization of Black Americans has been identified as a mental health disparity; however, few national studies have examined this disparity. This article reports on whether Black adults are hospitalized at a percentage higher than their population percentage and diagnostic differences between Black and White adults receiving services in state hospitals and other hospital inpatient settings. A subsample of adults ages 18 to 60 and older (N = 263,943) was drawn from the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration’s 2020 Mental health client-level data set. Univariate analysis found that the percentage of Blacks receiving services in state hospitals and other hospitals exceeded their percentage in the 2020 U.S. population (12%) by 19.7% and 9.5% respectively. Bivariate and regression analysis revealed an association between Black race and diagnoses of schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders in both hospital settings. Approaches for addressing disproportionate hospitalization and diagnostic bias are suggested.

title: New Perspectives on Gender Identity and Fertility Ideals in Australia
authors: Natalie Nitsche, Xioaxian Qiu, Ben Edwards, Daniela Grunow, Markus Hahn, Mary Lou Rasmussen
abstract: Binary conceptualizations of gender are not only scientifically obsolete; they also may obscure important gender variation in fertility motivations. We offer a novel perspective on gender and its link with fertility ideals by conceptualizing gender identity as a multi-dimensional latent construct. We use measures on self-rated masculinity, femininity and gender identity importance from the 2024 Australian Social Survey International-ESS (AUSSI-ESS) to identify latent gender identity profiles and examine their association with fertility ideals among people aged 18-39. While the categorical gender measure showed no differences between cis-men and cis-women’s fertility ideals yet much lower fertility ideals among non-binary/trans people, multi-dimensional profiles revealed more nuanced patterns. Among childless respondents, the masculine profile had a significantly higher ideal number of children (1.52) than the feminine (1.17) and gender diverse (0.95) profiles. Notably, the gender diverse profile, combining moderate levels of self-assessed femininity, masculinity, and gender identity importance, was the largest among the childless, comprising 43 percent of the sample. This indicates stark variation in fertility ideals withing cis-gender groups, which remains masked by classical categorical gender measures. Hence, multi-dimensional gender conceptualizations appear to provide not only more inclusive but also more precise insights into contemporary fertility preferences.

title: Policy or Polity? Experimental Evidence on Voters’ Information Priorities in European Parliament Elections
authors: Mathias Wessel Tromborg, Roman Senninger, Andreas Videbæk Jensen
abstract: European Parliament (EP) elections are among the world’s largest democratic events, but they are challenging for voters. Campaigns address issues from the future of the polity to specific policies, with the Parliament holding more power over the latter. To understand what information voters need to act in their interest, we examine what they prioritize when accurately informed about the EP’s powers. We use field experimental data from two Voting Advice Applications embedded on a major Danish news site during the 2024 EP election. Users were told that the EP has more influence over policy than polity issues, chose whether to receive advice based on policy or polity preferences, and stated their vote intention before or after receiving advice. Analyzing 66,000 users and a representative survey experiment (n=2,951), we find that citizens prioritize policy over polity information under accurate information. These results have important implications for representation research, media and politicians.

title: Feeling iffy about generative AI: Investigating Audiences’ Trustworthiness Perceptions of Task-Specific AI disclosures
authors: Nicolas Michael Mattis, Kimon Kieslich, Claes de Vreese
abstract: News organisations are experimenting with how to best integrate generative AI into their journalistic workflows. This raises questions about how AI use should be disclosed and how such disclosures affect readers. Prior research shows predominantly negative effects on perceived trustworthiness and credibility, but says little about how AI disclosures for different use cases compare to each other. In this study, we report the results of a conjoint experiment (N = 683) on the effects of task-specific AI disclosures on the perceived trustworthiness of news. While the magnitude of effects varies, we consistently find negative effects across all task-specific AI disclosures. However, moderation and cluster analyses suggest that these effects are not universal, but depend on individual-level characteristics that co-determine AI disclosure effects. By (1) highlighting important individual-level moderators such as respondents’ political position and their knowledge of journalistic AI, and (2) describing five distinctive preference profiles and their predictors, our results inform future research and help practitioners cater AI disclosures to particular groups of readers. To this end, we also situate our work within broader debates about what meaningful transparency should look like.

title: And Yet, Anger Moves: The Galileo Structure of Organizational Defense and the Denied Circulation of Workplace Anger
abstract: This paper develops a theoretical framework for understanding workplace anger not as an isolated individual emotion or a single episode of emotional expression, but as a social motion that circulates, is imitated, becomes morally justified, transforms into silence, and is institutionally denied. Previous studies have examined anger through emotion regulation, abusive supervision, workplace bullying, emotional contagion, social learning, organizational silence, and whistleblower retaliation. However, less attention has been paid to the structure in which anger visibly circulates within an organization while the organization refuses to recognize that circulation and instead problematizes the person who observes it. To address this gap, this paper proposes the concept of the Galileo Structure. The Galileo Structure refers to a defensive pattern in which, when an inconvenient movement, circulation, or transmission is observed, an authority system avoids substantive examination of that movement and instead frames the observer as deviant, disruptive, disloyal, or procedurally problematic. The phrase “Eppur si muove,” commonly attributed to Galileo, is used here not as a confirmed historical statement but as a cultural metaphor for denied motion. The central claim of this paper is that anger moved, but the organization could not acknowledge its movement. Recognizing the circulation of anger would connect a seemingly individual emotional problem to broader issues of managerial responsibility, evaluation systems, psychological safety, recurrence prevention, whistleblower treatment, and institutional defense. As a result, organizations may fragment the movement of anger into isolated incidents and shift attention from the observed structure to the observer’s attitude, procedure, loyalty, or alleged disruption of order. The paper further argues that anger does not necessarily disappear once it becomes visible as a problem. Instead, it may transform into silence, avoidance, information withholding, and relational exclusion. In this sense, silence is not always the opposite of anger; it can be anger’s second form. By connecting anger, silence, organizational defense, and the marginalization of the observer, this paper offers a conceptual framework for analyzing workplace harassment and institutional denial. Anger may lose its voice, but it continues to move.

title: Social Media, Public Perception, and Corporate Crisis Communication in Nigeria: A Multiple Case Study of Consumer-Led Brand Controversies.
abstract: The expansion of social media platforms across Nigeria has fundamentally altered the dynamics of corporate reputation management. Consumers now wield unprecedented power to shape public narratives about organizations through individual posts, videos, and reviews that can reach millions within hours. This paper examines how three Nigerian organizations responded to consumer-led crises amplified on social media and how those responses shaped public perception. Using a qualitative multiple-case study methodology grounded in Coombs' (2007) Situational Crisis Communication Theory (SCCT), the paper analyzes the cases of Nagiko Tomato Mix/Erisco Foods Limited (2023), Bon Bread/Food & Food Integrated Company Limited (2026), and Bolt Nigeria (2022–2025). The first two cases are characterized by defensive, legalistic responses, including consumer arrests, lawsuits, and police involvement that intensified public backlash and inflicted measurable financial and reputational damage despite both products subsequently receiving regulatory clearance. The third case demonstrates a contrasting approach: Bolt Nigeria responded to recurring consumer safety complaints through corrective action, safety infrastructure investment, and transparent stakeholder communication, achieving measurable platform improvements and sustained public trust. Findings suggest that consumer-led controversies on social media do not necessarily damage corporate reputation because of the allegations themselves; rather, public perception is significantly shaped by how organizations respond to those allegations. Legal-defensive responses in Nigeria's social media environment consistently amplify reputational damage, while corrective-action responses aligned with SCCT's rebuild cluster produce more favorable outcomes. The paper concludes with practical implications for Nigerian organizations and calls for greater investment in proactive crisis communication infrastructure.

title: Occupational Neoteny: Workplace Self-Domestication Failure, Untouchable Symbols, and the Preservation of Juvenile Affect Within Mature Authority
abstract: This paper theorizes occupational neoteny as an organizational process in which juvenile affective patterns are preserved within mature occupational authority. Although a manager or authority figure may possess professional competence, responsibility, status, and institutional power, their modes of anger, dependency, charm, recognition-seeking, self-defense, and pseudo-intimacy may remain emotionally juvenile when workplace environments fail to demand emotional maturation. Drawing on the biological concepts of neoteny and paedomorphosis, this paper uses neoteny not as a biological reduction of social behavior, but as a theoretical metaphor for the preservation of juvenile affect within adult authority. It further introduces the idea that workplaces function as local self-domestication devices. Schools, companies, and organizations do not merely transmit knowledge and skills; they regulate reactive aggression, impulsive expression, and self-centered behavior, thereby transforming individuals into cooperative social actors. However, when an authority figure becomes an untouchable symbol through awards, past achievements, charismatic legitimacy, rare positional status, or organizational recognition, this local self-domestication device begins to operate asymmetrically. Subordinates become over-domesticated through silence, compliance, self-suppression, and psychological shrinking, while the authority figure remains emotionally under-domesticated. Reactive anger, juvenile affect, neotenic charm, and self-defensive responses are not corrected but preserved within mature occupational power. The paper argues that occupational neoteny emerges from this asymmetrical failure of workplace self-domestication. Juvenile anger may be reframed as guidance, responsibility, seriousness, or strictness, while reducing subordinates’ voice and psychological safety. At the same time, neotenic charm—such as sweetness, vulnerability, playfulness, or pseudo-intimacy—may neutralize criticism and make coercive authority appear forgivable or “human.” When juvenile anger and neotenic charm combine with charisma, symbolic legitimacy, and organizational protection, they may become an imitable success style, developing into an Angry Monster or Rage Influencer. By framing occupational neoteny as an organizational developmental structure rather than an individual pathology, this paper connects research on abusive supervision, psychological safety, organizational silence, emotional labor, charismatic authority, cultural learning, and person–environment fit. It offers a theoretical framework for understanding how anger, charm, authority, silence, and turnover become structurally linked in workplaces where emotional maturation is demanded of subordinates but exempted for symbolic authority figures.

title: Using heteroskedasticity-consistent standard errors and the bootstrap for linear regression analysis in SPSS: A tutorial
authors: Hanna Rajh-Weber, Stefan E. Huber, Martin Arendasy
abstract: In the landscape of statistical software, from customizable programming-language-based to point-and-click systems, SPSS remains a popular choice among researchers. In SPSS (version 29), analyses with conventional methods, like ordinary least squares regression, can be easily performed. However, not satisfying assumptions like homoskedasticity or normality of the errors can lead to altered type I error rates or a reduction in statistical power. SPSS provides a multitude of alternative inference methods associated with linear regression, but many of them are not plainly locatable through the point-and-click menu. To facilitate data analysis when assumptions for conventional inference methods are not met, this tutorial provides applied researchers with a step-by-step guide for performing linear regression with heteroskedasticity- consistent standard errors (HC3 and HC4) and two different bootstrap resampling methods (pairs bootstrap and wild bootstrap). Each bootstrap method can further be combined with a bootstrap p-value, a percentile confidence interval, or a bias-corrected and accelerated confidence interval. The methods are then compared using a computer-generated data set. Although the main focus of this article is SPSS, a tutorial on how to do everything shown here in R (with custom functions) is included in the supplementary materials.

title: A practice-oriented guide to statistical inference in linear modeling for non-normal or heteroskedastic error distributions
authors: Hanna Rajh-Weber, Stefan E. Huber, Martin Arendasy
abstract: Selecting an appropriate statistical method is a challenge frequently encountered by applied researchers, especially if assumptions for classical, parametric approaches are violated. To provide some guidelines and support, we compared classical hypothesis tests with their typical distributional assumptions of normality and homoskedasticity with common and easily accessible alternative inference methods (HC3, HC4, and six bootstrap methods) in the framework of ordinary least squares (OLS) regression. The method’s performances were assessed for four different regression models with varying levels of non-normality and heteroskedasticity of errors and five different sample sizes ranging from 25 to 500 cases. For each scenario 10,000 samples of observations were generated. Type I error and coverage rates, power, and standard error bias were examined to assess the methods’ performance. No method considered here performed satisfactorily on all accounts. Using HC3 or HC4 standard errors, or a wild bootstrap procedure with percentile confidence intervals, could yield reliable results in many, but not all, scenarios. We suppose that, in the case of assumption violations, researchers might refer to a method that performed best in a scenario most similar to their data situation. To aid selection of an appropriate method we provide tables comparing relative performances in all considered scenarios.

title: Economic horizontal inequality is linked to conflict persistence, not onset: High-resolution evidence from satellite-derived wealth measures in Africa
authors: Connor T. Jerzak, Adel Daoud, Johannes Fagerlind
abstract: Conflict research links horizontal inequality—systematic inequalities between identity groups—to armed violence, but it remains unclear whether economic inequality predicts conflict outbreak or instead shapes conflict dynamics after violence becomes plausible. We revisit this question in Africa using high-resolution satellite-derived International Wealth Index estimates to measure economic horizontal inequality among politically relevant ethnic groups and local wealth shocks. In group-year models, economic horizontal inequality is not statistically associated with conflict onset, while political marginalization is a stronger predictor. By contrast, economic horizontal inequality is associated with active-rebel group-years and higher casualty intensity among active-conflict group-years. A spatiotemporal counterfactual analysis finds that dense local wealth-shock scenarios are not associated with more conflict events on average, but estimated conditional contrasts become positive in areas with large absolute economic horizontal inequality. Economic horizontal inequality may therefore matter less as a general trigger than as a condition shaping conflict persistence, severity, and shock vulnerability.

title: Umgang mit Emotionen von diskriminierten Personen in der Verbraucher:innen-Beratung: Eine qualitative Untersuchung von Beratungsstrategien
authors: ConPolicy Institut für Verbraucherpolitik, Marlene Münsch, Sophia T. Dasch, Julius Rauber, Jule Niemeyer, Tammo Schmidt
abstract: Die vorliegende qualitative Studie untersucht, wie Beratungsorganisationen emotionale Belastungen diskriminierter Verbraucher:innen wahrnehmen und in der Beratung bearbeiten. Ziel ist es, Einblicke in bestehende Beratungspraktiken, organisationale Herausforderungen und Entwicklungsbedarfe zu geben. Die Untersuchung basiert auf zwei methodischen Zugängen: einer Akteursfeldanalyse von 91 Beratungsorganisationen sowie 15 leitfadengestützten Expert:inneninterviews mit Beratenden aus verschiedenen Marktsektoren, darunter allgemeine Verbraucherberatung, Wohnungsmarkt, Finanzdienstleistungen, Gesundheitswesen, digitale Märkte sowie Kultur- und Freizeitmärkte. Die Interviews wurden inhaltlich-strukturierend ausgewertet. Die Ergebnisse zeigen, dass Diskriminierung in allen untersuchten Marktsektoren vorkommt und Verbraucher:innen entlang sozialer Kategorien wie Alter, Behinderung, Geschlecht, sexueller Orientierung, Herkunft oder Religion betrifft. In Beratungsgesprächen wird Diskriminierung jedoch selten ausdrücklich benannt, sondern zeigt sich häufig implizit in den geschilderten Anliegen. Wie offen sie thematisiert wird, hängt unter anderem vom Spezialisierungsgrad der Organisation ab. Emotionen sind ein zentraler Bestandteil der Beratungssituation. Beratende berichten besonders häufig von Wut, Angst, Verzweiflung, Scham und Traurigkeit. Positive Emotionen wie Hoffnung oder das Gefühl, verstanden zu werden, entstehen oft erst im Verlauf der Beratung. Emotionen beeinflussen damit sowohl das Erleben der Ratsuchenden als auch Verlauf und Dauer der Gespräche. Im Umgang mit emotionalen Belastungen nutzen Beratende unterschiedliche Strategien, darunter Empathie, Validierung, aktives Zuhören, Deeskalation, Wissensvermittlung sowie sach- und lösungsorientierte Unterstützung. Emotionale Anerkennung und Sachorientierung schließen sich dabei nicht aus; vielmehr kann das Aufgreifen von Emotionen eine Voraussetzung für erfolgreiche Problembearbeitung sein. Besonders bei Diskriminierungserfahrungen spielt Empowerment eine wichtige Rolle, um Handlungsmöglichkeiten aufzuzeigen und Ratsuchende zu stärken. Gleichzeitig wird der Umgang mit Emotionen und Diskriminierung meist erfahrungsbasiert, informell und über kollegialen Austausch erlernt. Verbindliche Leitlinien, fachliche Standards und Fortbildungen fehlen häufig. Hinzu kommen strukturelle Herausforderungen wie Zeit- und Personalmangel, Sprachbarrieren und komplexe Problemlagen. Insgesamt verdeutlichen die Befunde, dass Diskriminierung und Emotionen in der Verbraucher:innenberatung eine wichtige, aber oft implizite Rolle spielen. Die Ergebnisse sprechen für eine stärkere Verankerung diskriminierungs- und emotionssensibler Kompetenzen in Ausbildung, Fortbildung und Organisationsentwicklung sowie für eine engere Zusammenarbeit mit spezialisierten Beratungsangeboten.

title: Warum Beschäftigte KI am Arbeitsplatz seltener nutzen: Gründe für den Rückgang der beruflichen KI-Nutzung in Deutschland
abstract: Der Kurzbericht untersucht das Ausmaß und die Veränderung der beruflichen Nutzung künstlicher Intelligenz (KI) unter Erwerbstätigen in Deutschland. Grundlage ist eine quotierte Online-Befragung (CAWI) von rund 2.585 Erwerbstätigen. Im Mittelpunkt steht, wie sich die berufliche KI-Nutzung in den letzten drei Monaten verändert hat, welche Gründe für eine verringerte Nutzung genannt werden und wie eine Zu- oder Abnahme mit individuellen und organisationsbezogenen Merkmalen zusammenhängt. Die Mehrheit berichtet von einer gleichbleibenden Nutzung. Insgesamt überwiegen Zunahmen, während eine Verringerung nur einen kleinen Teil der Erwerbstätigen betrifft. Eine rückläufige Nutzung ist mit kritischeren KI-Einstellungen, geringerer subjektiver Kompetenz und organisationaler Unterstützung sowie mit fehlendem Mehrwert, unzuverlässigen Ergebnissen und Datenschutzbedenken verbunden. Zugleich zeigen sich soziale Unterschiede: Höherqualifizierte erhöhen ihre Nutzung eher, geringer Qualifizierte verringern sie vergleichsweise häufiger. Damit stellt sich die Frage, ob KI bestehende Ungleichheiten am Arbeitsplatz abbaut oder verstärkt.

title: Social and health risk factors for miscarriage: a population-based study using French healthcare records
authors: MC Compans, Hélène Malmanche, Heini Väisänen
abstract: Objective: To study social and clinical factors associated with (early or late) miscarriage, while accounting for births and induced abortions. Design: Population-based analysis of French healthcare records from 2015 to 2023. Subjects : Pregnancies among women aged 15-49 that resulted in birth, miscarriage or induced abortion. Main Outcome Measures: Predicted probabilities for a pregnancy to result in birth, induced abortion, or miscarriage, including early (<14 gestational weeks) or late (14-22 gestational weeks) miscarriage. The probabilities are estimated using multinomial regression models controlling for key socio-demographic and health factors. Results: Eight percent of the studied pregnancies were miscarriages and among them, seven percent occurred at a late gestational age. Adjusted results for socio-demographic factors show age below 19 or above 40 were associated with higher predicted probabilities for miscarriage: while the predicted probabilities were below 10% for the age groups in the middle, they were 14.0% [13.9%;14.2%] for adolescents and 16.0% [15.9%;16.1%] for those in their early 40s. There were no differences in overall miscarriage risk between rural, peri-urban and urban residential areas, or by financial precarity status, except for late miscarriages where risk was higher among those in a precarious financial situation. Regarding past reproductive history, miscarriage was more likely after the use of medically assisted reproduction, with the predicted probabilities for this group being 14.9% [14.7%;15.0%] compared to 7.5% [7.4%;7.5%] for those without. Previous births or induced abortions were associated with lower predicted probabilities of miscarriage, whereas previous miscarriages were associated with an increased probability of miscarriage (e.g. 12.5% [12.3%;12.8%] after multiple miscarriages and 7.3% [7.3%;7.3%] if no previous miscarriage). Chronic diseases were each associated with slightly higher predicted probabilities of miscarriage, with exceptionally high association for kidney failure (17.0% [15.1%;19.1%]). Conclusion: No social inequalities were observed for early miscarriage risk, but the risk of late miscarriage was more important for financially precarious than other women. Regarding health factors, chronic kidney disease was particularly strongly associated with elevated miscarriage risk, while all included chronic diseases increased the risk somewhat.

title: Die Rettungsmaschine. Sexarbeit, Anti-Trafficking und extraterritoriale Strafmacht in Südostasien. Eine theoretische Synthese.
abstract: This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of sex work and anti-trafficking power in Southeast Asia and argues that the region reveals US extraterritorial power in its fourth and most contradictory mode. Where a companion paper on the Global North found that power exercised through platform law (FOSTA-SESTA), a paper on the Global South through the PEPFAR antiprostitution pledge, and a paper on Eastern Europe through the abrupt withdrawal of funding, Southeast Asia shows it as evaluative power: the US State Department's annual Trafficking in Persons (TIP) Report ranks governments by tier and can trigger sanctions, rewarding the criminalisation, raids and rescue operations that Bernstein (2018) has theorised as carceral feminism and militarised humanitarianism. The paradox is that the same US power that financed harm reduction through PEPFAR simultaneously incentivises criminalisation through the TIP Report - while the strongest available evidence, a quasi-experimental study from East Java (Cameron, Seager and Shah 2021), shows that criminalisation increases sexually transmitted infections among sex workers by 58 percent. Drawing on this evidence, on Weitzer's (2023) ethnography of the Thai sex industry, and on Parmanand's (2019) study of the Philippine Sex Workers Collective, the paper makes three claims. First, the anti-trafficking architecture functions as a machine that produces the victims it claims to rescue, extending Agustin's (2007) concept of the rescue industry into its operating logic. Second, this machine structurally suffocates the self-organisation that could carry reform, because criminalisation prevents legal registration and funding. Third, Thailand's ongoing effort to repeal its 1996 Prostitution Act is the Southeast Asian counterpart to the Belgian and South African experiments, in which the sex worker organisation Empower explicitly demands decriminalisation rather than regulated legalisation. The evidence base is uneven: Thailand and the Philippines carry the analysis, while Vietnam appears only as an evidencebased contextual case.

title: The Rescue Machine Sex Work, Anti-Trafficking, and Extraterritorial Carceral Power in Southeast Asia. A Theoretical Synthesis.
abstract: This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of sex work and anti-trafficking power in Southeast Asia and argues that the region reveals US extraterritorial power in its fourth and most contradictory mode.
