title: The IP Paradox for New Zealand Scientific Start-Ups: Navigating Value and Impediment in Technology Transfer
abstract: This study examines how scientist-entrepreneurs in New Zealand experience and navigate intellectual property (IP) during the technology transfer process. Using interpretative phenomenological analysis, the study draws on semi-structured interviews with fourteen scientist-entrepreneurs, analysed via reflexive thematic analysis. The findings reveal a central IP Paradox; IP is simultaneously an indispensable prerequisite for technology transfer and a significant impediment. Useful theories, the Innovation Incentive Theory, Knowledge Spillover Theory of Entrepreneurship, and Resource-Based View, only partially explain this phenomenon, as managing IP introduces profound strategic tensions, costs, and uncertainties. The analysis elucidates the IP Paradox for scientific start-ups, which complements and adds on to these dominant theoretical frameworks, and then develops an IP Navigation Matrix as a sense-making and decision-support tool grounded in entrepreneurs’ lived experience. This provides a nuanced understanding of IP’s dual role, offering direct managerial and policy implications for improving technology transfer, supporting academic entrepreneurship, and fostering innovation in New Zealand and other small open economies. The findings also provide practical insights for IP practitioners advising scientific ventures in New Zealand. Keywords: intellectual property protection; scientific start-ups; patents; trade secrets; technology transfer; New Zealand

title: Every Methodology is a Weapon if you Hold it Right: Union-Led Well-Being Measurement as an Act of Collective Resistance
abstract: Workplace well-being surveys are typically led by employers in a hierarchical, top-down way, treating distress as a problem on the individual scale rather than a collective condition. Drawing on Marston et al.’s (2005) critique of hierarchical scale, this paper reimagines well-being measurement by developing a democratic practice of industrial participation and epistemic resistance that offers more horizontal points of entry. As part of a union-led case study at the University of Dundee, Scotland, we examine how a community-driven initiative co-designed and administered a well-being survey during a period of acute institutional crisis (N = 84). Survey findings indicated near-universal burnout with widespread physical and psychological ill-health, reports of excessive unsustainable workloads, low autonomy, and eroded organisational trust. The paper shows that that participatory data practices can represent horizontal infrastructures of care and that these practices work as both evidence of harm and as revolutionary theory in Harvey’s (2009: 152) terms: engaging with status quo ideas like well-being to provide “them with real content, and using them to identify real choices immanent in the present”. We draw on theories of pragmatism to discuss how radicalising well-being measurement extends industrial democracy by shifting the means of knowledge production: workers define and evidence their own realities, transforming surveys from tools of managerial oversight into practices of solidarity and weapons for resistance. To resist the use of well-being measurement as a tool of hierarchical government, we aim to show that every methodology can be a weapon if you use it right.

title: What makes or breaks newly-cohabs? Couples’ gender attitudinal pairings and their union trajectories
abstract: While declining marriage and rising cohabitation are not new, less partnering, more breakups, and rising singlehood are novel and poorly understood trends. How newly cohabiting couples' joint gender attitudes predict their relationship trajectories, and whether this varies across social contexts, is hence an important yet underresearched question. Using data from the German Family Panel (pairfam), we follow 1,297 newly formed couples, investigating how their gender attitudinal pairings evolve and predict breakups or marriage transitions, leveraging East-West German gender climate differences to assess contextual moderation. Our findings extend the literature in three ways. First, gender climate context matters for marriage transitions: among West but not East German couples, joint traditionalism is associated with more than twice the marriage transition rate of attitudinally mismatched couples. Second, attitudinal convergence among initially mismatched couples (the majority in our sample) emerges as the strongest predictor of both higher marriage rates and lower breakup risks across both regions. Third, the gender normative context appears to interact with attitudinal pairings in whether couples marry but not in whether they stay together. This suggests intra-couple psychological processes are a key predictor of family formation dynamics and deserve more attention in demographic research.

title: The Measurement Crisis in Cognitive Warfare Defence: Evaluating Single-Layer Countermeasures across Democratic Societies
abstract: State-sponsored cognitive warfare has emerged as a defining cross-national security challenge, systematically targeting the information environments of democratic societies and allied institutions. Yet despite the proliferation of defensive countermeasures since 2020, systematic evidence evaluation remains critically limited. This research examines four major individual intervention categories (prebunking and inoculation theory, artificial intelligence detection technologies, media literacy programmes, and rapid response systems) through structured evidence synthesis applying adapted GRADE criteria. Two research objectives guide the analysis: evaluating empirical evidence quality and identifying measurement limitations that systematically overestimate operational effectiveness; and analysing performance gaps between laboratory conditions and real-world deployment. Evidence reveals substantial variation in empirical support across countermeasure categories. Prebunking demonstrates the strongest evidence base, with large-scale field studies showing 55-60% improvements in manipulation detection. AI detection systems show 45-50% accuracy degradation from laboratory to operational conditions due to adversarial adaptation. The research identifies a fundamental measurement problem: over 89% of studies analysed measure capabilities rather than behaviours, creating systematic overestimation of intervention effectiveness. These findings provide evidence-grounded guidance for national security planners, defence ministries, and NATO member states allocating resources to cognitive defence, emphasising behavioural validation as a prerequisite for operational confidence.

title: Communal Activities as Intercultural Communication Strategies of Social Actors in Developing Social Cohesion in Sudiroprajan
authors: Buddy Riyanto, Andrik Purwasito, Andre Noevi Rahmanto, Sri Hastjarjo
abstract: This article examines communal activities as intercultural communication strategies used by local social actors to develop social cohesion in Sudiroprajan, Surakarta, Indonesia. Sudiroprajan is a multicultural urban neighborhood in which Javanese residents, Chinese Indonesians, Javanese-Chinese descendants, and communities with diverse religious, generational, occupational, and socio-economic backgrounds interact in everyday life. Using a qualitative approach within a constructivist paradigm and a grounded theory strategy, this study draws on observation, in-depth interviews, and documentation of community practices. The findings show that Grebeg Sudiro, Barongsai Macan Putih, neighborhood collective work, Kampung Pelangi, the Harapan Kita Waste Bank, the Suka Maju Women Farmers Group, and the Sinar Mentari Climate Village Program operate as communicative arenas where residents encounter difference, negotiate meanings, coordinate roles, reproduce local values, and create shared experiences. Social actors, including community leaders, neighborhood administrators, religious figures, women’s groups, youth, cultural activists, and environmental organizers, act as initiators, mobilizers, facilitators, mediators, and interpreters of meaning. The article argues that communal activities are not merely socio-cultural or environmental programs; they constitute an infrastructure of intercultural communication that transforms diversity into social capital, strengthens trust, and supports inclusive community identity.

title: Internal Dispossession: A Sovereignty-Grounded Theory of the Harms of American Indian Identity Theft
abstract: American Indian identity theft (self-indigenization, pretendianism) is usually treated as one person’s deception. I argue that it is something larger. This article presents Internal Dispossession Theory (IDT), a sovereignty-grounded theory of the harms of American Indian identity theft. Its premise is that identity theft is the latest form of colonial dispossession: as Native land and resources were taken, so too is the authority of tribal nations to say who belongs, relocated from the nation onto individuals, institutions, and markets. That relocation produces relational, structural, and epistemic harms that culminate in internal dispossession: the erosion of a tribal nation’s capacity to define itself, lived also by the individual Native person whose place a counterfeit takes. Stated as nine propositions and grounded in a sovereign autoethnography of a documented case, IDT names a contemporary colonial mechanism. Its remedy is not institutional gatekeeping but tribal self-determination.

title: Islam, Reason and Modernity in Colonial India: a Comparative Study of Sir Syed Ahmad Khan and Syed Ameer Ali
abstract: The paper examines the debates around reason and rationality in Islam, particularly in the works of Sir Syed Ahmad Khan and Syed Ameer Ali, locating their modernist articulation in the backdrop of socio-political context of colonial India. In the aftermath of the revolt of 1857, Muslims in India were subjected to institutional marginalisation, incarceration, civilisational stigma and intensified suspicion. As the orientalist discourse portrayed Muslims and Islam as an irrational, oriental and despotic tradition, it rendered Muslims political claims vulnerable to discrimination under colonial modernity. This crisis of political exclusion pushed for an articulation of Islam as a rational and ethical tradition. The paper argues that Sir Syed Ahmad Khan and Syed Ameer Ali reinterpreted Islam as a rational tradition which transcends mere theological category and served also as a political strategy of survival, locating Islam and Muslims as legitimate ethical civilisation. Sir Syed grounds his interpretation of Islam in a rationalist hermeneutics, through natural law and scientific reason which aimed at making Islamic tradition intelligible to colonial epistemology, enabling education, political participation and representation. Syed Ameer Ali while running on the similar thread, works along a parallel pathway of juridical and historical reasoning to defend Islam as a moral and constitutional epistemology. Though differing in methodology and approach, both thinkers rearticulated Islam as compatible with the changing world order. Through a comparative study of these two thinkers, the paper tries to identify the different and multiple rationalities within the framework of Islamic modernism in Colonial India. The paper states that this political idiom of survival and legitimacy works in extending and reshaping Muslim reformist thought, reoriented debate on constitutional politics, representation and institutional arrangement in colonial India. By foregrounding reason as a political strategy, the paper contributes to the intellectual history of Islam in South Asia and the ways through which modernist Islamic discourse negotiated through power, exclusion and belongingness under colonial rule in India. Keywords: Islam, Muslim, Colonialism, Modernity, Political exclusion, South Asia

title: SCIENTIFIC FACTS AS CIVILISATIONAL ACHIEVEMENTS: AN ASSESSMENT
abstract: Are scientific facts civilisational achievements? Answering this requires clarifying both terms. Drawing on the classical Islamic tradition, namely, al-Fārābī’s virtuous city and the later frameworks of Ibn Khaldūn, Osman Bakar, Wan Mohd Nor Wan Daud, and al-Attas, we argue that a genuine civilisational achievement must engage the physical, mental, and spiritual dimensions of human flourishing, and, on al-Attas’s view, must stand in some relation to revealed Truth. We then characterise a “scientific fact” not as plain truth but as a proposition that is provisionally true: empirically constrained, communally validated, and open to revision. Using three “outdated facts” Pluto’s demotion, the abandonment of the luminiferous aether, and the discovery of additional states of matter, we show that scientific facts are revisable in structurally different ways: definitional, ontological, and enumerative. The deepest layer, the constituent framework or worldview, is never delivered by experiment; it is inherited from the civilisation that produces the science. Every scientific fact is therefore a civilisational achievement, requiring the madīnah, the ummah of inquirers, and a budaya ilmu. But when scientific claims overstep their evidential station and smuggle in an inherited metaphysics, the Muslim community is under no obligation to receive that metaphysics as fact. This, I suggest, is the real meaning of al-Attas’s de-westernization of knowledge: to accept what the instruments have honestly read from the book of creation, while declining the unexamined worldview wrapped around it.

title: Psychological features of dispute content and public acceptance of AI in legal adjudication: evidence for systematic variation beyond individual differences
abstract: Public acceptance of artificial intelligence (AI) in legal decision-making has been primarily explained through individual differences in personality traits and general attitudes toward technology. However, emerging evidence suggests that contextual features of legal disputes themselves may systematically influence preferences for AI versus human adjudicators. Across two studies with Japanese participants (N = 1,384 and N = 596), we examined whether psychological characteristics of dispute content—beyond demographics and individual traits—shape acceptability judgments for algorithmic adjudication. Study 1 employed exploratory factor analysis on acceptability ratings across 46 legal dispute vignettes, revealing a robust two-dimensional structure distinguishing interpersonal-relational disputes (where human adjudicators were strongly preferred) from institutional-procedural disputes (where AI acceptance was comparatively higher, though not surpassing human preference in most cases). Study 2 replicated this dimensional structure in an independent sample and demonstrated that experimentally manipulated contextual features—emotional involvement and prototypicality—systematically modulated acceptability judgments, with effects varying by dispositional trust, AI-specific attitudes, and gender. AI-specific expectations emerged as the strongest predictor of acceptance (η2 = 0.252), and a three-way interaction among emotional involvement, gender, and prototypicality indicated that contextual effects are moderated by individual characteristics. These findings suggest that the psychological features of dispute content constitute an overlooked dimension in AI acceptance research, extending beyond technology acceptance models to fundamental questions about how individuals construe social problems and allocate adjudicative authority. We discuss limitations related to measurement approaches, alternative psychological mechanisms, and directions for future research employing real-world case materials and direct assessment of cognitive processes.

title: Research on the Communication and Synergistic Development of Yao Medical Culture and Industry: A Case Study of Liannan Yao Medicine in Guangdong Province
abstract: Against the backdrop of the "Healthy China" initiative and the rural revitalization strategy, the preservation and industrial development of Yao medical culture face both opportunities and deep-seated obstacles. Taking the Liannan Yao Autonomous County in Guangdong Province as a case study, this research systematically investigates the current state and challenges of the local Yao medicine industry through three methods: a questionnaire survey (238 valid responses), in-depth interviews (with 3 Yao medicine inheritors and 2 local cultural officials), and participant observation (15 days of fieldwork). The findings are threefold. First, while the local cultural identification rate with Yao medicine reaches as high as 78%, the actual utilization rate is only 55.5%, and the rate of choosing Yao medicine as the first option for medical treatment is as low as 3.6%, revealing a cognitive-practical paradox of "high identification, low consumption." Second, chi-square tests indicate a significant association between ethnic identity and usage behavior (χ² = 8.41, p < 0.01), as well as a significant divergence between trust and actual use (χ² = 28.94, p < 0.001). Third, the formation of this paradox can be attributed to a fourfold blockage: institutional access barriers, lack of product standardization, cognitive disconnection, and the strong substitution effect of modern medicine. The study proposes a five-dimensional synergistic development pathway—encompassing institutional supply, communication mechanisms, product transformation, cultural tourism integration, and industrial ecology—to transform Liannan's Yao medicine from a form of local knowledge into a competitive cultural and industrial asset.

title: An Analytical Framework for the Assessment of Employment Impacts of Energy Transition
authors: Rudeena Jabar, Anjali Sharma, Haritha Songola
abstract: The potential employment generation is an important co-benefit and policy motivation to pursue energy transition. Empirical evidence suggests that energy transition leads to net employment gains. However, the job estimates vary across studies due to methodological choices, underlying modelling assumptions and scope of analysis. In this study, we present a systematic review of the studies that have examined labor impacts of energy transition to understand the underlying mechanisms that impact employment estimates. Using the Context-Interventions-Mechanisms-Outcome framework, we selected 236 papers for the study. The paper identifies multiple factors that explain these variations: first, the type of assessment used in the study impacts the projected job numbers. Gross assessment (42% of studies in our sample) provide optimistic results without accounting for the potential job losses. Second, ambitious climate action scenarios in an economy leads to the increase in projected jobs. We find that 25% of studies are dependent on the scenario assumptions. Third, employment details such as employment factors, learning effects, job quality and skills determine the intensity of the job estimates. The review suggests that only 12% of the studies have included learning effects in their assessment and 15% have assessed the skill requirements for the manufacturing of renewables. Based on these limitations in the existing literature, we have developed an analytical framework that can serve as the benchmark for studying the labor impacts of energy transitions. The framework systematically identifies and highlights the importance of integrating different elements previously discussed in the labor assessment studies to avoid the over-estimation/underestimation of employment numbers.

title: When evaluation certifies international excellence: A category-validity test from Brazilian graduate education
abstract: This article examines a category-validity problem in indicator-driven research evaluation: whether the programs a national system certifies as internationally excellent in fact publish through internationally visible journal circuits. The Brazilian federal evaluation of graduate education uses CAPES scores 6 and 7 to designate programs equivalent to “international centers of excellence”, with Qualis Periódicos serving as the journal-classification instrument for the two periods analysed (2017--2020 and 2021--2024, the closing cycles of the Qualis era). I match program-reported articles by International Standard Serial Number (ISSN) to the 2024 SCImago Journal & Country Rank (SJR) database, using SJR coverage, language, and quartiles as provisional indicators of international circulation; unmatched articles remain in the denominator, since non-indexed output is part of the policy-relevant outcome. Linguistics/Literature serves as a critical case: a field with rich local research objects, many international publication outlets, low publication-cost barriers, and CAPES 7 ratings. Across both periods the certified-excellent stratum publishes a much smaller share of articles in English, appears far less often in SJR-indexed journals, and has a very low proportion of articles in Q1/Q2 quartiles when all journal articles are counted. The case illustrates how an indicator-driven evaluation regime can sustain a stable category of certified international excellence whose alignment with externally measurable publication circuits is weak, raising broader questions about category validity in evaluation regimes for non-anglophone systems.

title: Material, Epistemic, and Normative Institutional Orientations: A Common Vocabulary for Composing Social Theory
abstract: Integrating fragmented social theories requires a common vocabulary that is comprehensive, generatively irreducible, persistent, and configurable, so that theories can be mapped without distortion and composed across frameworks. This paper identifies Material (M), Epistemic (E), and Normative (N) institutional orientations as a triad that satisfy these requirements. These orientations arise through the coordination of actions motivated by actor-level drives, while remaining distinct from those drives. This paper develops configurational space and locates major types of social theory as projections of that space. A worked example connects Streeck’s deferral devices to Habermas’s instrumental rationality, demonstrating the framework’s compositional payoff.

title: Towards Robot Armies? On the Strategic Implications of Unmanned Ground Vehicles
abstract: Global militaries are increasingly fielding unmanned ground vehicles, but significant technical challenges continue to hinder widespread adoption. No study appears to have focused on the implications of UGVs for the strategic level of war for the United States military. This article first uses open-source research and qualitative reasoning to explore the key advantages and limitations with UGVs, then explores several areas where UGVs could have effects on the strategic level of war: supporting a new way of war, massed defense and deterrence, allied support, and low-commitment engagement and escalation. Overall, the article offers insights into how UGVs may impact military and security affairs beyond their narrow tactical use in combat.

title: Harvesting Employment: An Economic Analysis of Odisha’s ₹3,100 Paddy MSP, Rural Youth Livelihoods, and Reverse Migration Dynamics
abstract: Odisha’s announcement of procuring paddy at ₹3,100 per quintal under the Samrudha Krushak Yojana is a notable state level intervention in terms of price support for agriculture. The central miscall of the key paddy minimum support price of the state input assistance policy is therefore expandable to beyond farm-gate price realization. This paper studies whether a higher procurement-linked paddy price can bolster rural youth livelihoods, induce seasonal labour demand, and create conditions for partial reverse migration in Odisha. The paper examines a wide range of verified secondary evidence on paddy MSP, state policy on procurement, PLFS labour indicators, MGNREGA person-days, and trend in paddy procurement in Odisha. The econometric component makes use of a clearly labelled illustrative district-level dataset for all 30 districts of Odisha since complete district-level public data linking youth employment and reverse migration is not available. Through the application of descriptive statistics, growth-rate analysis, correlation analysis and OLS regressions, we estimate the associations with agricultural procurement intensity, rural wages, availability of MGNREGA, irrigation, youth employment and potential for reverse migration. The results indicate that procurement-intense districts have better rural livelihood indicators and more reverse migration potential, but this effect is contingent on irrigation, transparent procurement, buffer stocks, milling capacity and non-farm value chain employment. The ₹3,100 paddy procurement can give a fillip to rural employment and help in migrant reintegration but only when it comes with efficiency in procurement, crop diversification, development of rural enterprises, and skills formation directed to youth.

title: Digital Customer Service Adoption Barriers in Telecommunications: Evidence from a 2019 Arab Gulf Empirical Study
abstract: Digital customer service has become an important component of telecommunications transformation. However, the availability of digital channels does not automatically lead to frequent or sustained customer adoption. This paper develops an unpublished MSc Business and Management research project completed in 2019 into an applied research paper on digital customer service adoption barriers, focusing on a major telecommunications operator in the Arab Gulf, which remains anonymized for confidentiality reasons. The study used a quantitative survey design and collected 271 valid customer responses. The findings showed that digital channel adoption was still developing: 41.0% of respondents used digital channels frequently, 43.5% used them sometimes, and 15.5% had never used them. The main barriers were linked to limited awareness of digital channel benefits, incomplete availability of required functions, slow request completion, complexity of use, preference for human interaction, trust concerns, and cases where digital interactions did not fully resolve customer needs. The paper positions the 2019 study as an empirical baseline for understanding digital customer service adoption barriers during an important period of telecommunications digitalization in the Arab Gulf. It argues that improving digital adoption requires more than expanding the number of digital channels. Effective adoption depends on awareness, functional completeness, speed, trust, usability, service quality, and successful service completion. The findings are discussed at a sector level, with contextual relevance to the digital transformation direction that emerged in Saudi Arabia and the wider region following the launch of Vision 2030.

title: Development and Pilot Testing of myChoice: A Workplace Disclosure Decision Aid for Employed Parents of Children With Mental Health Disabilities
authors: Lisa Stewart, Claudia Sellmaier, Mafaz Cossio, Mauricio Morales
abstract: This study developed and pilot tested MyChoice, a workplace disclosure decision aid for employed parents of children with mental health disabilities. Sixty-five employed parents completed pre- and post-intervention measures of decisional conflict and decision self-efficacy. Participants reported significant reductions in decisional conflict and increased decision self-efficacy following use of the decision aid. Disclosure preferences remained stable, indicating improved decision quality without promoting a particular disclosure strategy. Participants also rated the intervention highly on acceptability and usability. MyChoice demonstrates preliminary feasibility as a social work intervention that supports client self-determination through structured decision support for complex work-family decisions. Future efforts should involve implementing decision aid as part of shared decision-making in mental health clinics and schools.

title: Comparing Large Language Models for Text Classification: Model Selection Across Tasks, Texts, and Languages
abstract: Large-scale text analysis has grown rapidly as an analytic method in the social sciences and beyond in recent years and recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have made automated text annotation increasingly viable. This paper focuses on the comparative viability of closed-source and open-source LLMS for text annotation, testing the performance of 32 different LLMs in text classification across a range of tasks, text types, and languages. Using data in seven languages across 10 country contexts, the results show considerable variation in model performance, highlighting that researchers should carefully consider model selection as part of their LLM-centered classification strategy. In general, the closed-source GPT-4 exhibits relatively strong performance across all classification tasks, while open-source alternatives such as LLama3 and Qwen2.5 also show similar or even superior performance on select tasks. Many smaller open-source models, however, provide relatively unsatisfactory performance on more complex and non-English language coding tasks. The tradeoffs inherent in the use of each model are therefore highlighted to allow researchers to make informed decisions about model selection on a specific task-by-task basis.

title: The Development of Inequality During Primary Education: Investigating the Genetic and Environmental Sources Underlying Learning Differences
abstract: Are differences in educational performance between pupils at the start of schooling – and their underlying genetic and environmental sources – reproduced, accumulated, or compensated over the primary school career? I investigate this using reading comprehension and mathematics test scores of over 4,000 same-sex and opposite-sex twin pairs, identified in the Netherlands Cohort Study on Education (Grades 1-5). Biometric latent growth models show, first, that most of the achievement inequality is already present at the start of education and is related to genetic variance. Second, these initial achievement differences are somewhat compensated over time, more strongly for mathematics than for reading. This leveling is related to decreasing genetic and environmental differences. Third, at the same time, new sources of achievement differences emerge during primary education that are unrelated to initial achievement. These new sources of differences are captured by variance in growth rates and are mostly genetic. As a result, although inequality linked to initial achievement decreases, the total systematic inequality in achievement increases over time because these new sources of differences outweigh the compensation of initial differences. In other words, over the course of primary education, inequality based on where children start remains prominent yet gradually decreases while ‘new’ inequality based on how fast children learn is simultaneously opens up.

title: Disruptions in Normalization: Reflexive Monitoring in Journalism Adaptation and Audience Collaboration
authors: Gregory P Perreault, Valerie Belair-Gagnon, Jennifer Henrichsen
abstract: In the adaptation of innovations, journalists often reflect on areas of concern that can lead to subsequent selection, a strategic process of diminishing the practice of a routine. Through the lens of normalization process theory and structuration theory, this article explores how journalists reflexively monitor innovations. The article also elaborates on the ways journalists evaluate disruptive circumstances that can lead them to select routines for denormalization. Using 44 interviews with journalists from the United States, this study argues that reflexive monitoring occurs through collaborations with audiences: a key to helping re-center journalists on the viability of a routine with existing commitments.

title: ChatGPT, Generative AI, and an Epistemic Opportunity for Journalistic Authority
authors: Gregory P Perreault, Seth Lewis, Maxwell Ely
abstract: Journalists have long been concerned about the boundaries of their craft, wary that their work might be replicated by actors who do not adhere to the profession’s normative values. Early artificial intelligence (AI) technologies seemed unlikely to meet journalism’s standards of precision and judgment, but the launch of ChatGPT in late 2022 signaled a shift toward generative AI, which directly challenges journalism’s central knowledge-creation practices—particularly writing. Employing journalistic authority as a conceptual framework, this study examines how journalists interpret and respond to this epistemic crisis through a two-stage qualitative analysis: (1) a thematic examination of metajournalistic discourse (n=94 articles) surrounding ChatGPT and related generative chatbots, and (2) in-depth interviews with practicing journalists (n=21) about their evaluation, adoption, and integration of generative AI into newsroom workflows. Ultimately, we argue that epistemic crises such as this may also be conceptualized as epistemic opportunities, offering journalists a chance to reinforce their professional boundaries and distinguish their craft from that of more peripheral actors.

title: Inheritance Taxation Around the Globe and Over Time: Revenue and Distributional Implications
authors: Twisha Asher, Luca Giangregorio, Salvatore Morelli, Manuel Schechtl, Francesca Subioli, Carolyn Fisher
abstract: This paper introduces a new harmonized global database on estate, inheritance, and gift (EIG) taxation, covering more than 170 countries, some with data going back to the 18th century, and all U.S. states from 2006. Despite substantial and persistent cross-country heterogeneity in tax design, we document a declining trend in the adoption and progressivity of EIG taxes since the 1980s. Using a two-way fixed effects (TWFE) framework complemented with an event-study approach, we find that a one percentage point increase in the top rate is associated with approximately 8% higher revenues between 1965 and 2022, with larger effects (9–17%) from 1995 through 2019. U.S. state-level estimates echo these results with a semi-elasticity of about 11%. An event-study analysis of 54 significant tax policy reforms shows no evidence of pre-existing differential trends and reveals dynamic effects: revenues decline by roughly 50% within four years following a cut of at least 10% in top rates, with symmetric effects for rate increases of similar proportion. Guided by a conceptual framework, we parse out the conditions that leads steady-state top wealth shares to fall when the average EIG tax rate increases. Using TWFE specifications with 5-, 10-, and 15-year lags, we find evidence consistent with the theoretical prediction. A one percentage point increase in the top marginal tax rate is associated with a 0.11-point decline in the Gini coefficient after 10 years, with consistent effects across top and bottom wealth shares. (Stone Center on Socio-Economic Inequality Working Paper)

title: Wealth Inequality Trends Around the World: A First View Leveraging Multiple Data Sources
authors: Franziska Disslbacher, Salvatore Morelli, Matteo Targa, Carolyn Fisher
abstract: Using the novel GC Wealth Project Data Warehouse, this paper shows that wealth inequality has increased since the early 2000s in roughly 60% of the countries in our sample, although trends are not uniform across countries. While the largest gains in wealth shares accrue to the top 10%, relative losses are borne by the bottom 50% and the middle 40% alike. We reveal substantial variation of estimates across data sources and units of analysis, which complicates international comparison of wealth inequality. Yet, core trends at the country level are generally robust. Individual-based series generally report higher inequality than household-based ones. Regional patterns reveal sharp increases in inequality in Latin America, North America, India, and China, while Europe and Asia-Pacific exhibit more stable trends. Incorporating Forbes billionaire data extends coverage to low-income countries and Africa—where, notably, billionaire wealth relative to GDP has remained stable, marking a distinct exception to global patterns. (Stone Center on Socio-Economic Inequality Working Paper)

title: Making Federalism Work (for Democracy)
abstract: Can American federalism serve as a check on central authoritarianism? As formal structures go, federalism’s effectiveness as an institutional constraint on central authoritarianism appears limited. Yet, as we argue in this article, the federal structure provides creative political actors with a repository of legal, fiscal, and organizational resources to contest authoritarian rule. Realizing this potential, however, will require rebuilding subnational civic infrastructures. Moreover, it will require state and local officials to employ their existing authority in new ways to expose the material consequences of authoritarian actions, increase the costs of these actions, and reduce the costs of dissent and resistance. We conclude that realizing federalism’s democratic potential depends on political choices rather than structural guarantees.

title: The Role of Editorial Boards in Shaping Scholarly Communication: A Large-Scale Mapping of Their Composition and Structure
authors: Evangelina Becerra Rodero, José Luis Ortega
abstract: Editorial boards play a central role in shaping scholarly communication by influencing what research is published and how disciplinary boundaries are defined. Despite their importance, large-scale, systematic evidence on their composition and structure remains limited. This study provides a comprehensive descriptive analysis of editorial boards across major scholarly publishers, with the aim of characterizing their organizational structure and the distribution of editorial positions. Using a corpus of over 810,000 editorial positions across more than 10,000 journals from 15 publishers, we examine board size and its relationship to journal output, develop a harmonized nine-class ontology of editorial roles, and analyze gender representation, institutional sectors, and geographic distribution. We also benchmark coverage against the Open Editors dataset, a previously released resource on editorial board composition. The results reveal stratified editorial structures dominated by operational roles, a positive association between board size and journal throughput, and a strong concentration of editors in academic institutions. Women represent approximately one-third of identified editors, and editorial presence is concentrated in the United States and Western Europe. This study provides large-scale descriptive evidence on editorial board organization and establishes an empirical baseline for future research on editorial governance, diversity, and the distribution of influence in scholarly publishing.

title: Towards a Colonial Civil Sphere: A Critical Systematic Review of Civil Sphere Theory in Colonial and Postcolonial Contexts
abstract: Jeffrey C. Alexander’s Civil Sphere Theory (CST) has become one of the most influential frameworks in contemporary cultural sociology for understanding how solidarity, inclusion, and democratic life are culturally constituted. Yet CST’s applicability beyond the Western liberal democratic contexts, from which it emerged, remains underexamined. This position paper undertakes a critical theoretical analysis of the scholarly literature engaging with Alexander’s The Civil Sphere (2006) and its subsequent extensions by drawing on a systematic review of 41 studies identified through Web of Science and Scopus. This analysis identifies four structural limitations of CST that render it inadequate for analyzing colonial and postcolonial contexts, namely, Euro-American centric universalism, a liberal democratic precondition, nation-state boundedness, and the erasure of colonial power relations. Based on these findings, the article proposes a Colonial Civil Sphere (CCS) framework, built upon the conceptual pillars of constitutive contradiction, structured partial inclusion, constrained civil agency, and coloniality as persisting structure. The CCS framework reorients analysis from CST’s civil repair to civil construction, offering a theoretically grounded approach for understanding how civil life is constituted with, under, and against colonial domination. This article concludes by outlining implications for future research on colonial and postcolonial civil societies in the Global South.

title: Gendered Expectations of Representation: Do Constituents Prefer Women to be Locally-Oriented?
abstract: Gender and politics scholars have found women legislators to be more district-oriented than their male colleagues, often arguing that this behavior reflects strategic efforts to secure reelection. Yet no research has directly examined whether citizens expect women representatives to prioritize district-related activities or reward them for doing so. I argue that gender stereotypes portraying women as more communal lead voters to expect greater attention to local representation from women and to penalize them more harshly when they neglect district responsibilities. To test these expectations, I field a survey experiment in which respondents allocate a hypothetical U.S. House member’s time across national policymaking, securing local funding, and constituent service. Respondents then evaluate the same representative after reading a news-story vignette describing them as either attentive or inattentive to district needs. Contrary to expectations, voters do not hold gendered expectations about representational priorities nor do they penalize women more for inattentiveness.

title: Climate Adaptation in the Lens of Frame Assemblages
authors: Vanja Falck, Ivan Puga-Gonzalez, LeRon Shults
abstract: People use frames, in Erving Goffman's sense, to make sense of encounters. Across Europe, the International Council for Local Environmental Initiatives (ICLEI) connects researchers, facilitators, implementers, and politicians on climate adaptation, yet knowledge reaches municipal practice only when participants adequately recognise each other's frames---which they often do not. Analysing 20 interviews, we show how frames embedded in assemblages (frame-assemblages) both obstruct and open up adaptation, with bottlenecks arising not only from scarce resources but from how situations are framed. We then outline a second use: derived from interview data, frame-assemblages act as empirically grounded, state-dependent filters on agents' capacities, offering a path from qualitative analysis to multi-agent social simulation.

title: Exhaustion, Not Burnout: The Case for an Upstream Mechanism as Stress Science's Core Construct
abstract: Burnout is among the most studied phenomena in occupational health, yet after five decades it lacks a consensual definition, a validated diagnostic threshold, and a demonstrated boundary from depression. This article approaches that difficulty not from burnout's weakness but from the strength of its core—exhaustion. Exhaustion is one of the best-established constructs in the psychological and stress sciences: it is measurable and performance-degrading in the cognitive-fatigue literature, it limits endurance in the psychobiology of performance, it prospectively predicts hard clinical endpoints such as myocardial infarction, and it is the most reliable and predictive dimension of every major burnout model. The instructive collapse of ego depletion—a depletion construct that failed rigorous replication—throws exhaustion's robustness into relief. I then pose a question the field has not squarely faced: is it adaptive to keep its most robust construct subordinate to a superordinate syndrome it cannot defend? I argue that it is not, and that the subordination is sustained by a taxonomic bid for scientific legitimacy and by a connotative accident of naming. I advance a constructive alternative that inverts the usual causal picture: rather than the terminal end of a stress chain, exhaustion is better understood as an upstream, trans-diagnostic, generative mechanism whose sustained depletion predisposes to depression, cardiovascular disease, and persistent cognitive impairment. Treating exhaustion, rather than burnout, as the field's core construct relocates explanatory weight onto defensible ground and dissolves several long-standing disputes.

title: Models of information transmission
abstract: This entry surveys formal theories of information transmission between interest groups and policymakers. It reviews models of cheap talk, information design, and disclosure, highlighting how strategic communication and institutional arrangements affect the credibility and influence of policy-relevant information.

title: Farmer-led innovations, government readiness, and agricultural diversification are key to prevent future food systems crises
authors: Thomas Cherico Wanger, Shakuntala Haraksingh Thilsted, Dong Sheng, Joachim von Braun, Shenggen Fan, Jean-Francois Soussana
abstract: Climate, economic shocks, and conflicts destabilize food systems and undermine all aspects of food security. Between 2016 and 2025, the level of food insecurity rose by 153%, primarily driven by droughts until 2019, then by the COVID 19 pandemic and the war in Ukraine. These tensions are amplified in face of the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, where the USA and Israel attack on Iran led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which 25% of global oil supply pass. Currently, 40% of petrochemicals and 15% of fossil fuels are now used in food systems for pesticide production, plastic packaging, and maintaining long haul cold chains. Disruptions to the flow of 30% of global fertilizer supply and 20% of liquefied natural gas, a key fertilizer feedstock, together with damage to infrastructure in exporting countries, are likely to constrain fertilizer availability for years to come. This will leave farmers in the 2026 crop growing season unable to have sufficient inputs and use their machinery for planting and harvesting. Immediate effects of the Iran war may be felt in vulnerable regions of the world already, with full ramifications manifesting in 2027, through increased food prices and food insecurity globally. Here, we argue that food systems dependence on fertilizer inputs can be generally reduced by enabling farmer-led adoption of diversified systems (e.g., legume rotations, agroforestry, biofertilizers and precision nutrient use) and locking in structural change through simultaneous government action. This is a critical addition to the currently dominating policy debate, where the argument is on fertilizers as strategic food-security infrastructure, with buffer reserves, low-carbon ammonia and tighter nutrient management.

title: La víctima frente a la caja negra: responsabilidad civil por daños de la inteligencia artificial tras la retirada de la Directiva europea (AILD)
abstract: La UE mató su Directiva de responsabilidad civil en IA (retirada formal en el DOUE, oct. 2025). ¿Qué pasa entonces cuando un sistema de IA te causa un daño? Dos países romanistas, misma UE, respuestas opuestas: Italia reconstruye el arsenal probatorio (Ley 132/2025 + decretos de junio de 2026: presunción de causalidad, exhibición técnica, acción directa contra el asegurador); España se abstiene (Proyecto de Ley Orgánica 2026: gobernanza y sanción, pero cero régimen civil).

title: A Yorùbá-Specified Framework for Ritual Deposition in West African Archaeology
abstract: Ritual deposition has long been recognized as an important dimension of religious practice in Yorùbá-speaking regions of southwestern Nigeria, but archaeological approaches to these deposits have relied heavily on generic models of structured deposition developed from European and Mesoamerican cases. Although these models have provided useful information, comparatively little work has examined whether their assumptions and interpretive logics adequately capture the regionally specific ritual principles through which Yorùbá practitioners generated material assemblages. This paper develops a Yorùbá specified analytic framework grounded in the concept of àṣẹ (the power-to-make-things-happen) as theorized by Abiodun, Abimbola, Olupona, Lawal, and Drewal and Mason. The framework formalizes a four process model (concentration, transfer, activation, dispersal) and integrates ethnographic insights from consultations with ritual specialists in three traditions (Ọ̀ṣun at Osogbo, Ògún at Ilé Ifẹ̀, and Ṣàngó at Ọ̀yọ́). These consultations substantively reshaped the analytic, which is presented explicitly as an author constructed synthesis rather than a Yorùbá emic taxonomy. The resulting protocol specifies both positive predictions and falsification criteria, enabling empirical differentiation between assemblages shaped by àṣẹ management practices and those predicted by alternative interpretive frameworks. Three published cases (Old Ọ̀yọ́, Obalara’s Land, and potsherd pavements) are presented as worked examples, with Old Ọ̀yọ́ serving as the lead case. Interpreted within the broader technological and ritual landscape of Yorùbá archaeology, the framework provides a baseline for future investigations of ritual practice, knowledge transmission, and regional variation in West African depositional traditions.

title: Understanding Behavioral Responses to Eco-Innovations: Insights from Innovator and Adopter Perspectives
abstract: This cumulative dissertation examines how individuals respond to eco-innovations, both as innovators within organizations and as consumers. Although a wide range of such innovations exists, many fail in practice due to insufficient market penetration or fall short of their expected resource-saving potential. The aim of this work is therefore to identify the conditions under which eco-innovations can lead to long-term, resource-conserving behavior. From a social-psychological perspective, the dissertation analyzes individuals’ motivations for engaging in resource-conserving behavior in the context of eco-innovations. Five studies investigate psychological mechanisms and behavioral patterns that influence the long-term success or failure of eco-innovations. The first part of the dissertation focuses on employees who proactively initiate resource-conserving innovations within their organizations. It examines whether such innovation-related behaviors promote or reduce subsequent environmentally friendly actions (so-called spillover effects). Three studies explore the psychological mechanisms underlying these follow-up effects. Central to this section is the development of a theoretical model to explain spillover effects following employee-driven resource-conserving innovations (Studies 1 and 2). The proposed model is empirically tested through an experiment (Study 3). The empirical findings suggest that engaging in resource-conserving innovation activities tends to reduce employees’ subsequent pro-environmental behavior in the short term. The dissertation thus contributes to explaining why eco-innovations may fail to realize their full resource-saving potential. Moreover, the findings highlight the importance of viewing employee-driven innovations not as static actions, but as dynamic processes in which potential follow-up effects must be taken into account. The second part of the dissertation investigates consumers’ adoption behavior toward digital eco-innovations such as energy monitoring applications (Study 4) and digital product passports (Study 5). It analyzes which types of information consumers particularly value and which factors influence their intention to use these innovations. The results indicate that perceived cost–benefit considerations, habits, governmental support, and cultural context are key factors shaping the adoption of these eco-innovations among broader user groups. Methodologically, the dissertation applies a theory-driven, quantitative-empirical multi-method approach. As a multidisciplinary study, it contributes to environmental psychology, innovation research, and information systems research, and provides practice-oriented implications for organizations, policymakers, and technology developers.

title: ALLIES Pilot: Identifying the barriers and facilitators for engaging adolescent boys in gender-based violence prevention research
abstract: The pervasiveness of gender-related violence among school-aged children and young people in the UK is well-evidenced (Barter et al., 2009; Ofsted, 2021; WEC, 2023). It is also well-accepted that involving boys and men is a fundamental step towards tackling this form of violence (Jewkes, Flood, & Lang, 2015). In the UK, the engagement of boys and young men in conversations around gender and violence against women and girls is now a core recommendation of both the Relationships and Sex Education statutory guidance (Department for Education, 2025), and the government Violence Against Women and Girls strategy (UK Government, 2025). While some research has begun to look at mechanisms for engaging young men to reflect on harmful gender norms, what it means to be a man in the UK today, and harmful behaviours/practices (O’Rourke & Haslop, 2023), very little work has considered the specific methods of engaging school-aged boys in such discussions in either research or education contexts. For this reason, this group can be considered a seldom-heard group within the field of gender-based violence prevention. In this preliminary report, we will discuss findings from a qualitative, pilot study comprising focus groups with young men and boys in Years 8-12 in UK secondary schools. Through our findings, we will explore the barriers and facilitators for engaging this group in gender-based violence research, and specific implications for research recruitment, retention, methods and design, ethics, and dissemination.

title: From the Economics of Choice to the Economics of Human Development: Reconsidering the Economic Agent in the Age of AI
abstract: Economics has developed sophisticated accounts of how individuals make choices, yet it has devoted comparatively little attention to how individuals become capable of choosing meaningfully. While the capability approach expanded economic evaluation beyond utility and resources, and provides conceptual space for further extension, it leaves the developmental formation of agency under-theorized: it specifies the opportunities available to persons without fully explaining how the capacity to exercise those opportunities is formed. This article argues that agency formation, the developmental process through which persons acquire the capacity for self-directed choice, should be treated as a distinct object of economic evaluation. Drawing on the capability approach as a normative foundation, Archer’s account of reflexivity as a developmental mechanism, and Akerlof and Kranton’s analysis of identity costs as constraints on self-direction, the article develops a three-dimensional evaluative criterion that assesses capabilities, agency, and agency formation simultaneously. The criterion evaluates economic institutions not only by the opportunities they generate but by whether they cultivate or suppress the developmental processes through which agency emerges. Artificial intelligence serves as a theoretical stress test: by increasingly substituting algorithmic systems for human deliberation, it exposes the inability of choice-centered evaluation to register developmental losses associated with the outsourcing of judgment. An application to formal schooling demonstrates how this evaluative approach generates conclusions that neither human capital theory nor the capability approach can produce on their own.

title: Generative AI Compresses Expertise Signals and Accelerates the Withdrawal of Genuine Contributors from Online Communities
abstract: We propose and analyze a mechanism by which generative AI discourages genuine human contribution across knowledge-intensive domains. When AI degrades the ability of audiences to distinguish high-quality authentic performance from AI-generated mimicry, the signal value of effortful contribution collapses. Agents with high ability — who invested most in authentic skill — face the sharpest devaluation of that investment and eventually withdraw. We formalize this signal compression mechanism in a dynamic model where signal environment quality decays exponentially following AI arrival. The model yields a closed-form withdrawal time that is increasing in ability, implying a survival gradient: more able agents disengage later. Crucially, the model predicts that the hazard of withdrawal accelerates over time and that this acceleration is positively moderated by ability — a delayed discouragement dynamic. We test these predictions using the complete contribution history of Stack Overflow, the world's largest technical knowledge platform, exploiting the late 2022 widespread availability of generative AI tools --- marked by the November 2022 launch of ChatGPT --- as a quasi-natural experiment. Using survival analysis on 24,304 active contributors, we find strong support for both predictions: a monotone survival gradient across pre-AI reputation deciles, and a positive and highly significant interaction between reputation and time, indicating that the hazard of withdrawal accelerates faster for higher-reputation contributors. These findings generalize beyond Stack Overflow: the same mechanism operates wherever AI compresses the signal value of human expertise, including education, scientific publishing, and labor markets. The welfare implications are severe --- AI induces a quality--quantity tradeoff in authentic human performance, raising per-contributor quality as low-ability agents exit while collapsing total authentic contribution volume.

title: How China-Origin Vision-Language Models Move from Refusal to Reframing in State Alignment
authors: GUANG YANG, Fengchen Liu, Alex Wang, Homa Hosseinmardi, Amir Ghasemian
abstract: State-aligned distortion has been documented in China-origin text-based large language models (LLMs), but whether, and in what form, it arises in multimodal systems has not been systematically examined. We construct a balanced benchmark of 200 core entries spanning ten politically sensitive topics, plus a seven-variant visual-abstraction probe, and run nine vision-language models (VLMs), seven China-origin and two non-China, across four elicitation paradigms and two prompt languages, yielding 21,708 trials. Each response is audited on six dimensions -- explicit refusal, information integrity, visual grounding, state-aligned framing, language consistency, and response length -- by two independent frontier LLM judges, validated against three human experts on a 200-trial sample. Measuring each dimension separately lets us decompose multimodal censorship into individual signals rather than a single refusal-based score; in particular, refusal and framing are measured independently, so a model can stop refusing while still reframing. We find that (i) Chinese-language prompting roughly triples the odds of state-aligned framing, within every model; (ii) China-origin models reframe more than non-China models (direction robust across judges and human raters; magnitude 1.6--3.2x); (iii) the effect is strongest in text-only political commentary (36.5%) and is gated by recognition of the depicted subject rather than pixel detail, persisting even at silhouette for iconic images; and (iv) across four Qwen generations, state-aligned framing rises while explicit refusal falls: censorship migrates from a visible act (refusal) to an invisible one (fluent reframing). We argue this shift to invisible reframing is fundamentally a problem of human-AI interaction: it removes the very signal users rely on to recognize that information has been withheld.

title: Heteronomía de la apertura. Financiamiento, colaboración y vías de acceso abierto en las universidades estatales chilenas (CUECH, 2015–2024) — Reporte Abierto, Ciencia Social Abierta 2026
abstract: Entre 2015 y 2024, las dieciocho universidades estatales chilenas (CUECH) publicaron la mayor parte de su producción en acceso abierto, pero esa cifra global esconde un reordenamiento interno que impulsa la pregunta de esta investigación. Mientras la vía diamante (sin costo para autores ni lectores) se mantuvo estable y la vía verde se contrajo, la vía dorada (la que cobra un cargo por procesamiento de artículo, APC) creció de forma sostenida. La pregunta es si ese desplazamiento se asocia a dos factores estructurales, la fuente de financiamiento y el perfil de colaboración internacional, o si responde a una decisión individual de quien investiga. A partir de datos abiertos de OpenAlex (97.236 publicaciones), el análisis muestra que la producción financiada por ANID o FONDECYT se inclina más hacia la vía dorada que la no financiada por esas fuentes, y que la coautoría con el Norte Global se asocia a más vía dorada y menos diamante que la producción exclusivamente chilena o con colaboración sur-sur. La vía de apertura se comporta más como una huella de quién financia y con quién se firma, lo que sugiere que el mandato de acceso abierto y el régimen de evaluación científica no se enfrentan, sino que se acoplan por la vía dorada. El trabajo es un ejercicio de ciencia abierta (hipótesis registradas en OSF, protocolo reproducible bajo estructura IPO y materiales públicos) y se ofrece como un recorte piloto de una investigación más amplia sobre el CUECH.

title: The Village Framework: A Maternal-Centered Model for Social Support, Clinical Care, and Structural Equity Across the Reproductive Continuum
abstract: The cultural proverb "it takes a village to raise a child" has shaped collective understandings of social support for decades. Yet a fundamental omission persists within this construct: the mother herself is rarely positioned as a primary beneficiary of the village she is expected to build. This paper presents The Village Framework, a maternal-centered conceptual framework proposing that the health and wellbeing of mothers across the reproductive continuum depend on the stability and interaction of three interdependent pillars: Social Support, Clinical Care, and Structural Support. These pillars rest on a Cultural and Equity Foundation that functions as the operative lens through which each pillar must be evaluated and delivered. Drawing on Bronfenbrenner's ecological systems theory, the Social Ecological Model, the Convoy Model of Social Support, the Fourth Trimester Framework, and the Reproductive Justice Framework, this paper synthesizes previously separate strands of theory into a single maternal-centered architecture. The framework advances three core propositions: first, that the mother must be recentered as a primary unit of care within the village construct; second, that the village is structurally unstable when any single pillar fails, regardless of the strength of the others; and third, that the period from preconception through the first year of the child's life is a foundational developmental window for both maternal and child health. A complete village is hypothesized to reduce maternal mortality risk, improve continuity of care, reduce postpartum mental health burden, strengthen mother-infant bonding, decrease perinatal substance use disorders, and create healthier developmental conditions for children. Four testable hypotheses are proposed to guide future empirical work. The framework is universal in scope and equity-conscious in application, with particular relevance to populations for whom systemic pillar failures have been most persistent.

title: Integrating Ground Penetrating Radar and GIS-Based Spatial Analysis for Archaeological Site Preservation: A Methodological Framework for Cultural Resource Management in the United States
abstract: Purpose – This paper proposes a methodological framework that combines Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR) survey with GIS-based spatial analysis to strengthen non-invasive documentation and risk assessment of archaeological sites managed under United States Cultural Resource Management (CRM) law. Design/methodology/approach – The framework is built from a critical synthesis of GPR archaeology, geospatial analysis, and CRM literature published between the late 1980s and 2024. A five-stage workflow is developed (data collection, GPR processing, GIS import, spatial analysis, preservation decision) and illustrated using published case studies of Indigenous mound sites, historic cemeteries, battlefields, and urban deposits. Findings – Existing CRM practice often treats geophysical survey and spatial analysis as separate technical steps rather than as a single decision-support system. When GPR amplitude time-slices are georeferenced and combined in QGIS with vulnerability layers such as erosion, planned development, and sea-level rise, managers gain a defensible, reproducible basis for triage and protective action without intrusive excavation. Originality/value – The paper contributes a CRM-oriented integration model, a tabular comparison of GPR antenna selection for common site types, and a vulnerability-weighting scheme that previous methodological work has not articulated together. It also identifies open-source, low-cost pathways suitable for under-resourced state and tribal preservation offices. Keywords Ground Penetrating Radar, GIS, QGIS, Cultural Resource Management, United States

title: TECHNOLOGICAL VARIATION IN IRON SMELTING IN SOUTHWESTERN TANZANIA: AN INTEGRATED XRF AND XRD ANALYSIS OF MALUNGU AND KATUKUTU FURNACES
authors: John Uche Ngonadi, Chioma Vivian Ngonadi, Abiodun Olayinka Ganiyu, Micheal C Atogu
abstract: Iron smelting was an important technological practice in precolonial southwestern Tanzania, where metallurgical production was organized through two structurally distinct furnace traditions: the tall natural-draft malungu furnaces of the Fipa and the shorter globular katukutu furnaces of the Nyiha in Isenzanya. Although the architectural characteristics of these traditions have been well documented, comparatively little archaeometric research has examined whether their typological differences are reflected in the composition of their production residues. This paper reports integrated X-ray fluorescence (XRF) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses of slag and furnace wall fragments from Miangarua (a malungu site in Sumbawanga) and Isenzanya (a katukutu site in Mbozi District). The slags from both sites are dominated by fayalite and a glassy silicate matrix and exhibit broadly comparable silica and alumina contents. However, Miangarua slag is enriched in MgO, K₂O, CaO, and P₂O₅, suggesting a greater contribution from fuel ash, whereas Isenzanya slag retains higher Fe₂O₃ concentrations, indicating less complete iron reduction or the use of richer ore feeds. The mineralogical and geochemical characteristics of the residues suggest that malungu and katukutu furnaces shared a common iron-silicate-based bloomery tradition, with differences reflecting variations in operational scale and smelting intensity rather than fundamentally distinct metallurgical recipes. Interpreted within the broader technological landscape of southwestern Tanzania, the findings provide a baseline for future archaeometallurgical investigations of technological variation and knowledge transmission in the Corridor region. Keywords: Archaeometallurgy; Iron smelting; Bloomery slag; XRF; XRD; Southwestern Tanzania
